EDCS-03000577.summary: This inscription records the filiation of Quintus Varius.

EDCS-03000578.translation: To Vitellia Sabina, daughter of Vitellius.
EDCS-03000578.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Vitellia Sabina, daughter of Vitellius.

EDCS-03000579.translation: Tillus, while alive, set this up for Quieta.
EDCS-03000579.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Tillus to Quieta, set up during his lifetime.

EDCS-03000580.summary: This inscription identifies Inna as the freedwoman of Caius Vinnus.

EDCS-03000594.text_edition: \n Marcus Veratius Mercatoris libertus Bassus Mercurio\n
EDCS-03000594.translation: Marcus Veratius Bassus, freedman of Mercator, to Mercury.
EDCS-03000594.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Marcus Veratius Bassus, freedman of Mercator, to the god Mercury.

EDCS-03000596.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, for Aphrodisius.
EDCS-03000596.summary: This is a dedication to the departed spirit of Aphrodisius.

EDCS-03000598.text_edition: \n Lucius Suestilius Luci libertus Aphrodisius medicus ocularius\n
EDCS-03000598.translation: Lucius Suestilius Aphrodisius, freedman of Lucius, eye doctor.
EDCS-03000598.summary: This inscription identifies Lucius Suestilius Aphrodisius, freedman of Lucius, as an eye doctor.

EDCS-03300643.text_edition: \n Domino nostro Flaviio sic Valerio Constantino bono rei [publicae nato ------\n
EDCS-03300643.translation: To our lord Flavius Valerius Constantinus, born for the good of the state...
EDCS-03300643.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Flavius Valerius Constantinus, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-03300651.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tertiniae Aureliae Marius Saturnin[us ------\n
EDCS-03300651.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Tertinia Aurelia... Marius Saturninus...
EDCS-03300651.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the departed spirit of Tertinia Aurelia, set up by Marius Saturninus.

EDCS-03300655.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Novelliae Eutychidis Threpte liberta de suo posuit\n
EDCS-03300655.translation: To the spirits of the underworld, Threpte, freedwoman of Novellia Eutychidis, set this up at her own expense.
EDCS-03300655.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Threpte, freedwoman of Novellia Eutychidis, funded by herself.

EDCS-03300656.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Statiliae Terti filiae Avitae Titus Severius Saturninus uxori pientissimae\n
EDCS-03300656.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Titus Severius Saturninus to his most devoted wife, Statilia Avita, daughter of Tertius.
EDCS-03300656.summary: This is a funerary dedication from a husband to his wife, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300661.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titi Epaticcii Voltinia Apri et Valeriae Frontinae\n
EDCS-03300661.translation: To the spirits of the departed, [dedicated by] Titus Epaticcius, son of Voltinia Aprius, and Valeria Frontina.
EDCS-03300661.summary: This is a funerary dedication, possibly for Titus Epaticcius and Valeria Frontina, indicating a family burial context.

EDCS-03300663.text_edition: \n Quinto Carantone Endami filio et uxsori sic Andolate Ateponis filiae\n
EDCS-03300663.translation: To Quintus Caranto, son of Endamus, and to his wife, Andolate, daughter of Atepos.
EDCS-03300663.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a couple, Quintus Caranto and Andolate, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300666.text_edition: \n Servilio Sacconis filio et Tertullae uxori \n
EDCS-03300666.translation: To Servilius Sacconis, son, and to his wife Tertulla.
EDCS-03300666.summary: This is a funerary dedication for a couple, Servilius Sacconis and Tertulla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300667.summary: This inscription appears to record the names of two individuals, Marcus Cornelius and Voltinia Fronto, possibly a couple or related family members.

EDCS-03300668.text_edition: \n Aemiliae Ateuloibitis filiae Bitugnatae ex testamento\n
EDCS-03300668.translation: To Aemilia Bitugnata, daughter of Ateulus, by her will.
EDCS-03300668.summary: This is a dedication made in accordance with a will for Aemilia Bitugnata, daughter of Ateulus.

EDCS-03300669.text_edition: \n @(obito) Lucio Pomptino @(obito) Caio Pomptino @(obito) Caio Pomptino\n
EDCS-03300669.translation: Upon the death of Lucius Pomptinus, upon the death of Gaius Pomptinus, upon the death of Gaius Pomptinus.
EDCS-03300669.summary: This inscription records the deaths of Lucius Pomptinus and Gaius Pomptinus, possibly a father and son or multiple individuals with the same name.

EDCS-03700530.translation: Voltinia Marino, daughter of Isaeus, twice.
EDCS-03700530.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or honor for Voltinia Marino, daughter of Isaeus, possibly indicating a repeated achievement or offering.

EDCS-03700533.text_edition: \n abuero X um ponam XX HS e reticulus e donis sacrificabo Mercurio XV HS stum et Maiae m sacrificabo X HS\n
EDCS-03700533.translation: From the purchased slaves, 20 sesterces; from the gifts, I will sacrifice to Mercury 15 sesterces, and to Maia, I will sacrifice 10 sesterces.
EDCS-03700533.summary: This inscription details a financial transaction and a vow to sacrifice to Mercury and Maia, likely a private religious dedication.

EDCS-03700572.text_edition: \n Uti voverat animo libens merito Neroni Limetio\n
EDCS-03700572.translation: As he had vowed in his spirit, willingly and deservedly, to Nero Limetius.
EDCS-03700572.summary: This inscription records a vow made willingly and deservedly to Nero Limetius, possibly a dedication or thank-offering.

EDCS-03700578.text_edition: \n Pio Felici pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate iterum pater pa[triae ------\n
EDCS-03700578.translation: To the most holy and fortunate pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the second time, father of the fatherland...
EDCS-03700578.summary: This inscription is a dedication, likely to an emperor, referencing religious and political titles.

EDCS-03700589.text_edition: \n Lumpha sic A III A III AAulis Atinis Cilici\n
EDCS-03700589.translation: Lumpha, son of Aulus, son of Aulus, from Atina in Cilicia.
EDCS-03700589.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name and ancestry of an individual from Cilicia.

EDCS-04900651.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely part of a dedication or ownership mark, mentioning Gaius Iuventius.

EDCS-04900652.text_edition: \n Garum vetus flos excellens sumaur CXXV Luci i Polyanthi Maurionis\n
EDCS-04900652.translation: Excellent old garum, the flower, 125 [units], of Lucius Polyanthus Maurio.
EDCS-04900652.summary: This is a label for a product, likely garum (fish sauce), indicating its quality and the producer.

EDCS-04900653.text_edition: \n Miscellum Portuense MVS excellens LXXX Gai Publici Saturisci\n
EDCS-04900653.translation: Miscellum Portuense, excellent MVS, 80 [units], of Gaius Publicius Saturiscus.
EDCS-04900653.summary: This is a label for a product, possibly wine or a food item, indicating its origin, quality, and producer.

EDCS-04900655.text_edition: \n Cnaeus A Ingenuus vixit annos V mensem semissem\n
EDCS-04900655.translation: Gnaeus A Ingenuus lived for 5 years and half a month.
EDCS-04900655.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the name and very young age at death of Gnaeus A Ingenuus.

EDCS-04900666.text_edition: \n Vitiocelo Donnia Gai liberta Vera votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-04900666.translation: Vitiocelo, freedwoman of Donnia, Vera, fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-04900666.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Vera, a freedwoman.

EDCS-04900668.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Flavi Veruclae Erotis Imperatoris Caesaris Flavi Veruclae plumbum germanum\n
EDCS-04900668.translation: Of the Emperor Caesar Lucius Flavius Verus Erotis, of the Emperor Caesar Flavius Verus, genuine lead.
EDCS-04900668.summary: This inscription seems to be a label or mark associated with lead, possibly referencing Emperors Lucius Flavius Verus and Flavius Verus.

EDCS-04900669.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Arminiaco sic pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XX imperatori III consuli III divi Antonini filio divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti\n
EDCS-04900669.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus Armeniacus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 20th time, imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva.
EDCS-04900669.summary: This is an extensive titulary inscription for Emperor Marcus Aurelius, listing his official titles and divine ancestry.

EDCS-04900670.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Aurelio Vero Augusto Arminiaco sic Parthico maximo tribunicia potestate VI imperatori III consuli II divi Antonini filius divi Hadriani nepoti divi Traiani Parthici pronepoti divi Nervae abnepoti\n
EDCS-04900670.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Aurelius Verus Augustus Armeniacus, Parthicus Maximus, with tribunician power for the 6th time, imperator for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, son of the deified Antoninus, grandson of the deified Hadrian, great-grandson of the deified Trajan Parthicus, great-great-grandson of the deified Nerva.
EDCS-04900670.summary: This is an extensive titulary inscription for Emperor Lucius Aurelius Verus, listing his official titles and divine ancestry.

EDCS-04900671.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pertenaci sic Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis imperatori III consuli II patri patriae Glanienses\n
EDCS-04900671.translation: To Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pertinax Augustus, Arabicus, Adiabenicus, Parthicus, pontifex maximus, holding tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 2nd time, father of the fatherland. From the people of Glanum.
EDCS-04900671.summary: This inscription records an acclamation of Emperor Septimius Severus by the people of Glanum, likely during his reign (193-211 AD).

EDCS-04900672.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of the camp. From the people of Glanum.
EDCS-04900672.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Julia Domna, mother of Emperor Caracalla and wife of Septimius Severus, by the people of Glanum, likely around the early 3rd century AD.

EDCS-04900673.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Augusto Imperatoris Caesaris Luci Septimi Severi Pii Pertenacis sic Augusti Arabici Adiabenici Parthici filio Glanienses\n
EDCS-04900673.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, son of Emperor Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus. From the people of Glanum.
EDCS-04900673.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Caracalla, son of Septimius Severus, by the people of Glanum, likely during Caracalla's reign (198-217 AD).

EDCS-04900695.text_edition: \n Imperatore Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Augusto IIII et Balbino consulibus Lucius Vireius Satto Viennae\n
EDCS-04900695.translation: During the consulship of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus for the 4th time and Balbus. Lucius Vireius Satto dedicated this in Vienna.
EDCS-04900695.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Lucius Vireius Satto in Vienna during the consulship of Caracalla and Balbus, dated to 212 AD.

EDCS-04900701.translation: To Gaius Messius Balbus, in accordance with his will.
EDCS-04900701.summary: This inscription records a dedication made according to the will of Gaius Messius Balbus, likely in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-05000587.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Hygiae Iu<l=I>ius <T=I>hal<l=I>us patronae karissimae posuit \n
EDCS-05000587.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Iulius Thallus set this up for his dearest patroness Julia Hygia, by will, and arranged for its placement; the space was granted by decree of the decurions; he ordered it to be made for himself by will.
EDCS-05000587.summary: This funerary inscription records a monument set up by Iulius Thallus for his patroness Julia Hygia, following her will and a decree of the local council, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-05000589.text_edition: \n Marti Beladoni Paternus Quintuli votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-05000589.translation: To Mars Beladonus. Paternus Quintuli gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-05000589.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Paternus Quintuli to the god Mars Beladonus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-05401072.translation: Flavius Montanus and Vol(tinia) Vocontius, a discharged soldier of the cohort of Ligurians, made this while alive.
EDCS-05401072.summary: This inscription records a monument erected by Flavius Montanus and Voltinia Vocontius, a discharged soldier, indicating a private dedication, possibly from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07000728.text_edition: \n Aulo Pompeio Auli filio Sabatina Pio aedili Kareia Sexti filia Ingenua mater testamento fieri rogavit Aulus Kareius Amomus heres ponendum curavit locus datus decreto decurionum testamento sibi fieri iussit\n
EDCS-07000728.translation: Aulus Kareius Amomus, the heir, arranged for this to be placed, as requested by his mother, Kareia Ingenua, daughter of Sextus, for Aulus Pompeius Pius, son of Aulus Sabatinus, aedile; the space was granted by decree of the decurions; he ordered it to be made for himself by will.
EDCS-07000728.summary: This inscription details the posthumous erection of a monument for Aulus Pompeius Pius by his heir, following the wishes of his mother and a decree of the town council, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07400561.text_edition: \n Itinius Cadirati Iovi votum libens merito\n
EDCS-07400561.translation: To Jupiter. Itinius Cadirati gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-07400561.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Itinius Cadirati to Jupiter, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-07400564.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Assius, son of Voltinia, likely from the Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-07600295.translation: Tilius, son of Erei, of the Pollia tribe.
EDCS-07600295.summary: This is a brief inscription identifying Tilius, son of Erei, and his tribal affiliation (Pollia), likely a formal record or ownership mark.

EDCS-08300452.summary: This is a dedication or construction inscription stating that Quintus Iulius Tric was the maker of the object or structure.

EDCS-08400380.text_edition: \n Tiberio Gavio et Mariae Satullae uxori et Pettidiae Tigridioni contubernali\n
EDCS-08400380.translation: To Tiberius Gavius and his wife Maria Satulla and his partner Pettidia Tigridio.
EDCS-08400380.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating a monument to Tiberius Gavius, his wife Maria Satulla, and his partner Pettidia Tigridio, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08400381.translation: To the Divine Shades of Titus Careius Ianuaris.
EDCS-08400381.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring the departed Titus Careius Ianuaris, dedicated to the Manes (spirits of the dead).

EDCS-08400382.text_edition: \n Sextus Decidius Prisci libertus V IIIIIIvir Augustalis ex testamento suscepto legavit\n
EDCS-08400382.translation: Sextus Decidius, freedman of Priscus, an Augustalis priest of the college of the IV Viri, bequeathed this by his will.
EDCS-08400382.summary: This is a funerary inscription stating that Sextus Decidius, a freedman and Augustalis priest, left a bequest by his will, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08400762.translation: For [---] / [---] bull of [---] / Publius Gargilius Ho[---] / priest of the college [---] / [---]DE[---]
EDCS-08400762.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely a dedication related to a taurobolium (bull sacrifice) and mentioning a priest, Publius Gargilius, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08400799.text_edition: \n Marco Octavio Nepoti ob votum iuventutis coniugem Foro duceret ipsa die vitam perdidit vixit annos XVIII Marcus Octavius Valerianus et Iulia Sempronia infelicissimi parentes in doloris sui solatium sic filio dulcissimo et sibi vivi memoriam fecerunt\n
EDCS-08400799.translation: To Marcus Octavius Nepos, in fulfillment of a vow of youth. His wife, while leading him in procession on the very day, lost her life. He lived 18 years. Marcus Octavius Valerianus and Iulia Sempronia, his most unhappy parents, in solace of their grief, made this memorial to their dearest son and to themselves while alive.
EDCS-08400799.summary: This is a poignant funerary and memorial inscription for Marcus Octavius Nepos, who died at 18, dedicated by his parents who also commissioned this monument for themselves, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08400828.translation: For the health of Antoninus, he performed a taurobolium for the goddess Mother Ideae.
EDCS-08400828.summary: This is an inscription recording a taurobolium (bull sacrifice) performed for the health of Antoninus, dedicated to the goddess Mother Ideae, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08400937.translation: To the Mother of the Gods, the Great Ideae, Lucius Decimius Pacatus and his wife Coelia Secundina, on account of the sacred taurobolium.
EDCS-08400937.summary: This is a dedication inscription for a taurobolium (bull sacrifice) performed for the Great Mother Ideae, by Lucius Decimius Pacatus and his wife Coelia Secundina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08401011.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Tito Flavio Titi filio? Agemacho patri Flavia filia eius Titus Flavius Agemachi filius bene de se merito fecerunt\n
EDCS-08401011.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Flavia, his daughter, and Titus Flavius Agemachus, his son, made this for their father Titus Flavius Agemachus, son of Titus, who deserved well of them.
EDCS-08401011.summary: This funerary epitaph was erected by children for their deserving father, Titus Flavius Agemachus.

EDCS-08401017.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus i Getuli Pederotis filio annorum XVII mensium IX Getulia Tertiola mater piissimo\n
EDCS-08401017.translation: To the Divine Spirits. For Getulius, son of Pederotis, aged 17 years and 9 months. Getulia Tertiola, his most devoted mother, made this.
EDCS-08401017.summary: This funerary epitaph records the death of Getulius, aged 17 years and 9 months, dedicated by his mother.

EDCS-08401029.translation: Sacred to the Divine Spirits. Gaius Manlius Mercurius lived for fifty years.
EDCS-08401029.summary: This funerary epitaph records the lifespan of Gaius Manlius Mercurius, who lived for fifty years.

EDCS-08401039.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mani Servili Marcionis Octaviana marito piissimo\n
EDCS-08401039.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Octaviana for her most devoted husband, Mani Servilius Marcion.
EDCS-08401039.summary: This funerary epitaph was dedicated by Octaviana to her devoted husband, Mani Servilius Marcion.

EDCS-08401040.text_edition: \n Spinter Decimi Pompei Fileti servos sic hic quiesquit\n
EDCS-08401040.translation: Spinter, slave of Decimus Pompeius Filetus, rests here.
EDCS-08401040.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Spinter, a slave belonging to Decimus Pompeius Filetus.

EDCS-08401046.text_edition: \n Memoriae Cai Vectici Neonis Vecticia Eucarpia alumno dulcissimo vixit annos XI menses VII dies XIII\n
EDCS-08401046.translation: In memory of Caius Vecticius Neon. Vecticia Eucarpia made this for her most sweet foster-son, who lived 11 years, 7 months, and 13 days.
EDCS-08401046.summary: This funerary epitaph commemorates Vecticia Eucarpia's foster-son, Caius Vecticius Neon, who died at the age of 11 years, 7 months, and 13 days.

EDCS-08401218.translation: For Quintus Vetinius Eunoetus, who lived 15 years and 3 months. Vetinii Hermes and Acte, parents, made this for their most devoted and dearest son, and for Hermais his sister, and for their freedmen and freedwomen and their descendants.
EDCS-08401218.summary: This funerary epitaph was erected by parents and a sister for Quintus Vetinius Eunoetus, who died at 15 years and 3 months, and also includes descendants.

EDCS-08401247.translation: To the deified Claudius, in accordance with the will of Lucius Valerius Placidus.
EDCS-08401247.summary: This dedication to the deified Claudius was made according to the will of Lucius Valerius Placidus.

EDCS-08401260.translation: For the Good Goddess. Attice, freedwoman of Caiena Prisca, minister.
EDCS-08401260.summary: This inscription records Attice, a freedwoman and minister of the Good Goddess, who belonged to Caiena Prisca.

EDCS-08401278.text_edition: \n Cominio filio Claudia Bono Agricolae Laelio Apro praefecto cohortis tertiae Bracaraugustanorum tribuno legionis I adiutricis procuratori Augustorum ad annonam provinciae Narbonensis et Liguriae praefecto alae miliariae in Mauretania Caesariensi naviculariorum marinorum Arelatensium corpora quinque patrono optimo et innocentissimo\n
EDCS-08401278.translation: For Cominius, son of Bonus Agricola, [and] Laelius Aper, prefect of the third cohort of Bracaraugustani, tribune of the I Adiutrix legion, procurator of the Augusti for the grain supply of the province of Narbonensis and Liguria, prefect of the milliary ala in Mauretania Caesariensis, patron of the five maritime shipowners of Arelate, the best and most innocent patron.
EDCS-08401278.summary: This inscription honors Cominius, son of Bonus Agricola, for his distinguished military and administrative career, including roles as prefect, tribune, procurator, and patron of shipowners.

EDCS-08401596.translation: May peace be with you. Vibia Eromene made this for Simplicius, her incomparable husband, with whom she lived for 3 years, as a memorial.
EDCS-08401596.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated by a wife to her husband, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500444.text_edition: \n Sapeoni cera Pylades nica Luci Pylades nica Luci N TV\n
EDCS-08500444.translation: Sapeon, Pylades the dancer (or servant) of Lucius, Pylades the dancer (or servant) of Lucius.
EDCS-08500444.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or memorial, possibly related to a freedman or slave named Pylades, and is difficult to date precisely due to its fragmentary nature.

EDCS-08500445.summary: This is a fragmentary epitaph recording the lifespan of an individual named RIAEECI, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500448.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iovinus pater et Leucadius mater filio pientissimo qui vixit annis Leontidi II et menses VIII dies XV\n
EDCS-08500448.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Jovinus the father and Leucadius the mother made this for their most dutiful son, Leontidus, who lived 2 years, 8 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-08500448.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young child, erected by his parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500451.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Achiropoio Iulia Servata ex visu\n
EDCS-08500451.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Achiropoius. Iulia Servata from a vision.
EDCS-08500451.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely set up by a woman named Iulia Servata after a divine vision, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500460.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to the god Mercury, its context and date are unclear.

EDCS-08500461.text_edition: \n Octavio Publi filio Mellito tribuno militum Octavius Fuscus pro fratribus\n
EDCS-08500461.translation: Octavius Fuscus for his brothers to Octavius Mellitus, son of Publius, tribune of the soldiers.
EDCS-08500461.summary: This is a dedication possibly commemorating military service, made by Octavius Fuscus for his brothers in honor of Octavius Mellitus, a military tribune, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500462.summary: This is a brief votive dedication, recording the fulfillment of a vow to an unspecified deity, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500705.text_edition: \n Iuliae Drusillae Germanici Caesaris filiae Tiberius parenti numinis honore delato posuit\n
EDCS-08500705.translation: Tiberius placed this in honor of Iulia Drusilla, daughter of Germanicus Caesar, a revered goddess.
EDCS-08500705.summary: This is a dedication honoring Iulia Drusilla, daughter of Germanicus Caesar and sister of Caligula, likely set up by Tiberius, dating to the early Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500872.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. ...di Athenodori / ...us Sigerus and / ...ofilla, parents / ...made this while alive.
EDCS-08500872.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a deceased person, with the monument erected by their parents who are still alive, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08500903.translation: To the divine Augustus, to the Mother of the Gods, for the safety of the Emperor Marcus Aurelius [[Commodus]] Antoninus Pius Felix, they made a tauropolium: Sextus Publicius [---]anus and [---]iana.
EDCS-08500903.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a tauropolium to the divine Augustus and the Mother of the Gods for the safety of Emperor Commodus, likely dating to his reign (177-192 CE).

EDCS-08500934.text_edition: \n BeratiaIIPVRIIIAI Proxsumas sic sibi et suis\n
EDCS-08500934.translation: Beratia Proxsumas, for herself and her family.
EDCS-08500934.summary: This very brief inscription likely records a private dedication or memorial by Beratia Proxsumas for herself and her household.

EDCS-08501001.translation: To the Mother of the Gods, the Great Idaean, for the safety of the divine house, they made a tauropolium and a criopolium: Aulus Pompeius Avitianus and Claudia Firmina, with Aulus Titus Phronimus, the priest, officiating.
EDCS-08501001.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a tauropolium and criopolium to the Great Idaean Mother of the Gods for the safety of the imperial family, with prominent individuals and a priest involved, likely dating to the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501081.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Annia Pupa, to her beloved grandson Caius Annius Atilaianus, aged 22.
EDCS-08501081.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Caius Annius Atilaianus at age 22, set up by his grandmother Annia Pupa.

EDCS-08501125.translation: Namuta made this monument for Minuta, her daughter, for herself and [---] her husband; this monument shall not follow the heir.
EDCS-08501125.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a monument by Namuta for her daughter Minuta and her husband, specifying that the monument is not hereditary.

EDCS-08501152.text_edition: \n Marcus Valerius Paulinus iugarius uxori karissimae\n
EDCS-08501152.translation: Marcus Valerius Paulinus, the iugarius, to his most beloved wife.
EDCS-08501152.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Marcus Valerius Paulinus, identified as a iugarius, to his wife.

EDCS-08501263.translation: To the Eternal Fires, Julius Placidianus, clarissimus vir, prefect of the praetorian guard, set this up in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-08501263.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument to the Eternal Fires by Julius Placidianus, a high-ranking official (praetorian prefect), in fulfillment of a vow, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501279.translation: To the Mother of the Gods Magna Idaea, sacred, they made a taurobolium with their beasts for sacrifice and all the paraphernalia: Lucius Dagidius Marius, perpetual pontiff of the city of Valentia, and Verullia Martina and Verullia Maria, their daughter, for the safety of the Emperors Philippus Augustus and Augustus, and of Otacilia Severa Augusta, mother of the emperor and the camp, with the priests officiating: Junius Titus, decemvir, of the people of Arausio, and Castricius Zosimon, of the people of Alba, and Blattius Paterno, of the people of Vocontii, and Fabricius Orfitus, of Liber Pater, and the other assisting priests, they fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly in the place where it was founded, on the day before the Kalends of October, when Philippus Augustus and Titianus were consuls.
EDCS-08501279.summary: This extensive inscription records a taurobolium dedication to the Mother of the Gods Magna Idaea for the safety of the Emperor Philippus Arabs and his family, performed by civic and religious leaders from various cities, dating precisely to September 30, 244 CE.

EDCS-08501280.translation: For the safety of the Emperor, he made a taurobolium from his own resources: Attius, son of Attianus.
EDCS-08501280.summary: This inscription records a private taurobolium performed by Attius, son of Attianus, for the safety of the Emperor, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501281.translation: To the Mother of the Gods, for the safety of the Emperor, they made a taurobolium: Titus Helvius Marcellinus and Valeria Decumilla, in fulfillment of a vow, with Attius, son of Attianus, as priest.
EDCS-08501281.summary: This inscription records a taurobolium dedication to the Mother of the Gods for the Emperor's safety, performed by Titus Helvius Marcellinus and Valeria Decumilla, with Attius Attiani as priest, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501370.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus liberorum ac coniugibus Publici Callisti et ipsius consecratum cum besse vineae arepennis ex cuius reditu omnibus annis prolibari volo ne minus XV vini sextariis exactis hic tumulus heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-08501370.translation: Consecrated to the Divine Shades, to his children and wives by Publius Callistus and to himself, together with three-quarters of an arpent of vineyard, the income from which I wish to be used each year to draw at least fifteen sextarii of wine; with this stipulated, this tomb will not pass to an heir.
EDCS-08501370.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Publius Callistus, establishing conditions for the upkeep of his tomb and vineyard income, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501425.translation: To the Divine Shades. Iulianus, son of Avitus, dedicated this to his dearest wife, Severinia Attica, beneath the measuring line.
EDCS-08501425.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Iulianus to his wife Severinia Attica, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501426.translation: To Diana Tifatina, Marcus Iccius Mumm(ius) dedicated this.
EDCS-08501426.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Diana Tifatina by Marcus Iccius Mumm(ius), likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501431.translation: To Mercury, Taurus Veratius willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-08501431.summary: This is a votive inscription to Mercury by Taurus Veratius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501466.translation: For the safety of the Emperors and for the divine house, Caius Valerius Urbanus(?), priest, and Caius Flavius Restitutus(?) performed a taurobolium and a criobolium of the Great Mother of the Gods Idaea.
EDCS-08501466.summary: This is a record of a taurobolium and criobolium ritual performed by two priests for the well-being of the Emperors and the imperial house, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-08501503.translation: To the Great Mother of the Gods Idaea, for the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Commodus Antoninus Augustus Pius and for the divine house of the colony Copia Claudia Augusta Lugudunensis, Quintus Aquius Antonianus, perpetual pontifex, performed a taurobolium, begun according to the prophecy of Pusanius Iulianus, archigallus, on the 12th day before the Kalends of May and completed on the 9th day before the Kalends of May, with Lucius Eggius Marullus and Gnaeus Papirius Aelianus as consuls, under the direction of Aelius Castrensis, priest, and with Albius Verinus as the tibicen.
EDCS-08501503.summary: This inscription records a taurobolium ritual dedicated to the Great Mother of the Gods Idaea in Lugudunum, performed by Quintus Aquius Antonianus for the safety of Emperor Commodus and the city, with specific dates and officials mentioned, dating to 185 AD.

EDCS-08501532.translation: Sacred to Apollo, from a vow. Gaius Virius Victor and Lucius Virius Vitalis willingly and deservedly fulfilled (their vow).
EDCS-08501532.summary: This is a votive inscription to Apollo by Gaius Virius Victor and Lucius Virius Vitalis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501536.translation: To Fortuna, from a vow. Lucius Terentius Sabinus.
EDCS-08501536.summary: This is a votive inscription to Fortuna by Lucius Terentius Sabinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501537.translation: A statue of the Genius. Namerius Euprepes, magister of the hastiferi, gave as a gift.
EDCS-08501537.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a statue of the Genius by Namerius Euprepes, an official of the hastiferi, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501539.translation: Sacred to Juno Regina Augusta. Titus Cassius Eros and his sons Cassius Priscus, Euphemus, and Secundus.
EDCS-08501539.summary: This is a sacred dedication to Juno Regina Augusta by Titus Cassius Eros and his sons, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501544.translation: To Mars. Sextus Contessius Verus, aedile.
EDCS-08501544.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the god Mars by Sextus Contessius Verus in his capacity as aedile.

EDCS-08501564.translation: To Afer, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-08501564.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by someone named Afer.

EDCS-08501569.translation: To Germanicus Caesar, son of Emperor Tiberius Augustus, grandson of the deified Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Julius.
EDCS-08501569.summary: This inscription honors Germanicus Caesar, providing his full lineage up to Julius Caesar.

EDCS-08501570.translation: To Drusus Caesar, son of Emperor Tiberius Augustus, grandson of the deified Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Julius, holding the tribunician power for the second time, consul for the second time.
EDCS-08501570.summary: This inscription honors Drusus Caesar, detailing his imperial lineage and his consulships and tribunician power.

EDCS-08501571.translation: To Gaius Caesar, son of Germanicus, grandson of Emperor Tiberius Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Augustus, Germanicus, the pontifex, quaestor.
EDCS-08501571.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Caesar (Caligula), detailing his imperial lineage and his religious and administrative offices.

EDCS-08501572.translation: To Gaius Caesar, son of Germanicus, grandson of Emperor Tiberius Augustus, great-grandson of the deified Augustus, Germanicus, the pontifex, quaestor.
EDCS-08501572.summary: This inscription honors Gaius Caesar (Caligula), detailing his imperial lineage and his religious and administrative offices, similar to EDCS-08501571.

EDCS-08501575.translation: To our lord Flavius Constantinus, the greatest and invincible Augustus, by virtue most strong and piety most clement, Marcus Alfius Apronianus, vir perfectissimus, praeses of the province of Flavia Viennensis, devoted to his divine majesty.
EDCS-08501575.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Marcus Alfius Apronianus, a high-ranking official, to Emperor Constantine, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-08501579.text_edition: \n Caio Iulio Pacatiano viro egregio procuratori Augustorum nostrorum militiis equestribus perfuncto procuratori provinciae Osrhoenae praefecto legionis Parthicae procuratori Alpium Coritt<i>arum adlecto inter comites Augggustorum nnnostrorum procuratori pro legato provinciae Mauretaniae Tingitanae colonia Aelia Augusta Italica patrono merentissimo\n
EDCS-08501579.translation: To Gaius Julius Pacatianus, an outstanding man, procurator of our Emperors in equestrian military service, procurator of the province of Osrhoena, prefect of the Parthian legion, procurator of the Cottian Alps, enrolled among the companions of our Emperors, acting governor of the province of Mauretania Tingitana, a most deserving patron of the colonia Aelia Augusta Italica.
EDCS-08501579.summary: This extensive inscription details the distinguished career of Gaius Julius Pacatianus, listing his various procuratorial and prefectural appointments under the Roman Emperors, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501598.translation: To Sextus, son of Sextus, Laetus, aedile, by the urban plebs (?).
EDCS-08501598.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Sextus Laetus, an aedile, possibly sponsored by the urban plebs, with a lacuna for the specific deity or purpose.

EDCS-08501601.translation: Attia Priscilla for herself [and...] on account of whose dedication she gave sportulas (cash gifts) of two denarii for the dendrophori.
EDCS-08501601.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Attia Priscilla for herself and possibly others, involving a distribution of cash for the dendrophori on the occasion of a dedication.

EDCS-08501603.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Quintus Connius Sauria, a priest of the college of Six, an Augustalis. Quintus Connius Aper, to his most devoted father and his grandfather, a priest of the college of Six, an Augustalis.
EDCS-08501603.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Quintus Connius Aper for his father and grandfather, both priests of the college of Six and Augustales, dating to the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501618.translation: To Sextus Marius Navus, aedile. Decimus Marius Martinus ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-08501618.summary: This is a funerary inscription honoring Sextus Marius Navus, an aedile, with the monument erected by Decimus Marius Martinus, likely his son.

EDCS-08501623.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Sollias Fida. Titus Cafatius Cosmus, a priest of the college of Six, an Augustalis, set this up for his dearest wife and himself while alive. To the spirits of the departed. To Sollias Fida. Titus Cafatius Cosmus, a priest of the college of Six, an Augustalis, set this up for his dearest wife and himself while alive.
EDCS-08501623.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Titus Cafatius Cosmus, a priest of the college of Six and an Augustalis, for his wife Sollias Fida and himself while alive, likely in the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501637.translation: To the spirits. By Caius Gessius Miccio. The associates, from their own funds.
EDCS-08501637.summary: This is a funerary dedication set up by the associates of Caius Gessius Miccio, funded by themselves.

EDCS-08501638.translation: Thraex Gratus Cethegianus, 17 years old, is buried here. Ferox set this up.
EDCS-08501638.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Gratus Cethegianus, a Thracian gladiator, who died at 17, with the monument erected by Ferox.

EDCS-08501642.translation: To the spirits of the departed and eternal memory. Lepidus Rufus, an anuboforus, who lived 40 years, 9 months, 3 days. His mother Palea to her most devoted son.
EDCS-08501642.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lepidus Rufus, an anuboforus (a type of priest or official), with his mother Palea dedicating the monument.

EDCS-08501643.translation: To the spirits of the departed and eternal peace. To Lucilius Metrobus, nicknamed Sapricius, stator of the community of Vienne, who lived 38 years, 2 months. Divicia Domitiola, mother, for her husband most dear and incomparable, arranged for this to be set up, and his sons dedicated it under the axe.
EDCS-08501643.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucilius Metrobus, a stator of Vienne, set up by his wife Divicia Domitiola and sons, likely in the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501644.translation: Grammaticus. To the spirits of the departed. To Lucius Marinius Italiscensis. Sollia Annia to her dearest husband, and Marinius Claudianus to his most devoted father. They dedicated it under the axe.
EDCS-08501644.summary: This is a dual funerary inscription dedicated by Sollia Annia to her husband Lucius Marinius Italiscensis and by Marinius Claudianus to his father, likely in the 2nd-3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501647.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Pompeia Cocca Andebrocirgis of the Sequani.
EDCS-08501647.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Pompeia Cocca Andebrocirgis, possibly of the Sequani tribe.

EDCS-08501650.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Valeria Iada Vocontia, who died at 27 years of age. Lucius Livius Severus to his dearest wife, for her exceptional merits, and for himself while alive and for his descendants.
EDCS-08501650.summary: This is a funerary inscription set up by Lucius Livius Severus for his wife Valeria Iada Vocontia, who died at 27, and for himself and his descendants.

EDCS-08501651.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Publius Vettius Gemellus, a sagarius of Romanes, lived 21 years, 8 months, 10 days. His father, Publius Vettius Profuturus, to his most dutiful son. May the earth lie lightly upon you.
EDCS-08501651.summary: This is a tombstone for Publius Vettius Gemellus, set up by his father, dating from Roman Imperial times.

EDCS-08501653.translation: A sagarius ordered this to be made in his will.
EDCS-08501653.summary: This fragment records a funerary commission made by a sagarius in his will, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501661.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Auxanon, 3 years and months. From Crescentius and Gemina, his parents.
EDCS-08501661.summary: This is a tombstone for Auxanon, set up by his parents, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501662.translation: [...] for a short time, my daughter [...] whose last rites I, Bellator, father, wickedly [...] and dedicated under the ash tree.
EDCS-08501662.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a funerary dedication by a father, Bellator, possibly regarding his daughter, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501665.translation: To Marcus Boius, son of Sextus, of the Voltinia tribe, Didianus. The heirs, in accordance with the will.
EDCS-08501665.summary: This funerary inscription records the heirs of Marcus Boius Didianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501670.translation: To the shades of the underworld and to the memory of Cominia Marcellina. Claudius Claudianus, her son, to his most dutiful mother.
EDCS-08501670.summary: This tombstone is dedicated by Claudius Claudianus to his mother, Cominia Marcellina, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501671.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Cominia Severina, a most obedient and modest woman. Lucius Tertinius Sextus, husband, to himself and to her, alive, and dedicated under the ash tree.
EDCS-08501671.summary: This funerary monument was dedicated by Lucius Tertinius Sextus to his wife Cominia Severina and to himself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501675.translation: To the shades of the underworld. Titus Couxolli Vitalis. The heirs arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-08501675.summary: This tombstone records that the heirs of Titus Couxolli Vitalis arranged for his monument, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501676.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Decidius Crescentius. Decidia [...] dia to her most dutiful son and to herself, alive.
EDCS-08501676.summary: This funerary inscription records Decidius Crescentius and his mother Decidia, who also set up the monument for herself, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501682.translation: To the shades of the underworld. To Fabia Semnes. Aulus Iulius Epitynchanus, to his wife.
EDCS-08501682.summary: This is a tombstone set up by Aulus Iulius Epitynchanus for his wife, Fabia Semnes, dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501685.translation: To the departed spirits. Graecus, deceased, aged 22. Donnia Modesta [placed this] for her pupil.
EDCS-08501685.summary: A funerary dedication by Donnia Modesta for her deceased pupil Graecus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501693.translation: To the departed spirits. For Julia Theagenis, daughter of Iulia Epicarpia.
EDCS-08501693.summary: A funerary dedication by Iulia Epicarpia for her deceased daughter Iulia Theagenis, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501695.translation: To the departed spirits and for eternal memory. Priminius Placidus made [this] for his most cherished wife, most honorable matron, most loving mother, most dear parent, Julia Severina, and also for his daughter [---].
EDCS-08501695.summary: A funerary dedication by Priminius Placidus for his wife Julia Severina and their daughter, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501699.translation: Aulus Lucilius Cantaber [erected this] for himself while alive.
EDCS-08501699.summary: A funerary inscription set up by Aulus Lucilius Cantaber for himself during his lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501705.translation: To the departed spirits. Marcus Magius Sotericus, nicknamed Hilarius, the friends' beloved, [erected this] for himself while alive, to be a memorial. Good journey [to the afterlife] for the living. [---]
EDCS-08501705.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Magius Sotericus, also known as Hilarius, for himself while alive, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period, with extensive, largely illegible funerary formulae.

EDCS-08501713.translation: To the departed spirits. For T. Pollinius Saturninus and Iulia Secundella. Pollinius Privatus [placed this].
EDCS-08501713.summary: A funerary dedication by Pollinius Privatus for T. Pollinius Saturninus and Iulia Secundella, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501720.translation: To the departed spirits and for the eternal memory of Sabina Ceria Asiatica, alive. Her mother [erected this].
EDCS-08501720.summary: A funerary dedication by the mother for Sabina Ceria Asiatica, intended to commemorate her while alive and after death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501721.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et quieti aeternae Titi Virei Mansueti signo Florenti defuncti nnorum II mensium V dierum XVIII Vireius Sapricius ettia Mansueta parentes infe[licissimi ------\n
EDCS-08501721.translation: To the departed spirits and eternal peace for Titus Vireius Mansuetus, nicknamed Florentus, who died aged 2 years, 5 months, and 18 days. Vireius Sapricius and Etia Mansueta, [his] most unhappy parents [erected this].
EDCS-08501721.summary: A funerary inscription by the parents Vireius Sapricius and Etia Mansueta for their young son Titus Vireius Mansuetus, dated by his age and likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501722.translation: To the departed spirits. Fortunatus placed [this] for Saturnina.
EDCS-08501722.summary: A funerary dedication by Fortunatus for Saturnina, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501727.translation: To the departed spirits and eternal peace. Cassia Fortunata, daughter, and Cassia Lais [placed this] for their husband [and] father Silvanus Fortunatus, an excellent man, a sarcophagus for himself while alive, and for themselves, next to his tomb, according to his wishes.
EDCS-08501727.summary: A funerary inscription by two women, Cassia Fortunata and Cassia Lais, for Silvanus Fortunatus, including a sarcophagus for themselves, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08501728.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Suca Vallo Atimitio Perpetuus and Ateia Atimitus Ermetio, sons and heirs, erected this for their most devoted father, Sextus Sucarius Atimitionis, and for their dearest wife Sucaria Perpetua.
EDCS-08501728.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by sons for their deceased father and wife, dating likely to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-08501730.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Taia Epagatho erected this for her husband and for her son Lucius Tai Onesiphorus, aged 5 years, and for her dearest descendants. And for Lucius Tai Epagatho, deceased, aged 23 years, her most devoted son.
EDCS-08501730.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by a wife and mother for her deceased husband and two sons, one very young and the other a young adult.

EDCS-08501735.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Catia Bubate placed this stone, dedicated under the axe, for her most devoted daughter Titia Catia, who died aged 8 years, 5 months, and 8 days, and for herself while alive.
EDCS-08501735.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young girl, erected by her mother who also provided for herself, indicating a date before the late 3rd century CE due to the 'sub ascia' dedication.

EDCS-08501741.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Duronia Ianuaria erected this for her excellent husband Sextus Valerius Sabinus. Valerius Bellinus and Bellicus [erected this] for their excellent patron.
EDCS-08501741.summary: A funerary epitaph for a husband, erected by his wife, and also honoring a patron by his freedmen.

EDCS-08501743.translation: Lucius Valerius Vitalis erected this for himself and his family while alive.
EDCS-08501743.summary: A funerary epitaph set up by Lucius Valerius Vitalis for himself and his relatives during his lifetime.

EDCS-08501748.translation: Uccius Eucarpus and Polycarpus erected this for [her] eternal rest and to the spirits of the departed while alive.
EDCS-08501748.summary: A funerary epitaph dedicated by two individuals, Eucarpus and Polycarpus, for someone named Uccius, while they were still alive.

EDCS-08501749.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Marcus Sergius Octavius [erected this] for his most holy mother Vennonia Iarilla.
EDCS-08501749.summary: A funerary epitaph erected by a son for his mother.

EDCS-08501750.translation: To the spirits of the departed and for eternal memory. Veteria Catiola and Pompeia [---] and Titus Non[ius] and [---] and Emione had placed and dedicated under the axe for their daughter Veteria Merceria, who lived 27 years, 4 months, and 21 days, and for themselves.
EDCS-08501750.summary: A complex funerary monument, likely dating to the 3rd century CE, where multiple family members honored a deceased daughter and also provided for themselves, with a dedication 'sub ascia'.

EDCS-08501754.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aulus Vinnius Gallus [erected this] while alive.
EDCS-08501754.summary: A funerary inscription recording Aulus Vinnius Gallus providing for his own burial while alive.

EDCS-08501755.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aulus Vinnius Gallus erected this while alive.
EDCS-08501755.summary: A funerary inscription recording Aulus Vinnius Gallus providing for his own burial while alive, identical to EDCS-08501754.

EDCS-08501767.translation: To the departed spirits. After [his son] T...camus died and [his son] T...meius, his daughter [M?]eia made [this]. To the departed spirits. To [his] sister.
EDCS-08501767.summary: A funerary dedication to deceased family members, including a sister, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08501773.translation: To the departed spirits. [Dedicated by] I[ul]ia, sister.
EDCS-08501773.summary: A funerary dedication by a sister to her deceased sibling, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08600486.text_edition: \n Attiae Luci filiae Paterclae flaminicae perpetuae gratuitae decreto ordinis Iuliensium Aptensium ob liberalitates patris eius qui praeter cetera CCCtrecenta milia HS rei publicae IIIIIIIvirorum reliquit ad ludos sevirales in perpetuum celebrandos Daphnion libertus locus datus decreto decurionum\n
EDCS-08600486.translation: To Attia Patercla, daughter of Lucius, perpetual and honorary flaminica, a Daphnion, freedman, was granted a place by decree of the order of the Iulian Aptensians on account of the liberality of her father, who besides other gifts left 300,000 sesterces to the state, for the celebration of the sevirate games in perpetuity.
EDCS-08600486.summary: A decree honoring Attia Patercla, perpetual flaminica, for her father's generous donation to fund perpetual games, from Apt, likely 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08600487.text_edition: \n Ordo sanctissimus Quinto Avilio Quinti filio Sennio Palatina Cominiano in honorem patris eius Quinti Avili Hyacinthi quod is praeter liberalitates spectaculorum quae sponte ededit sic vel postulata non negavit velis novis sumptu suo in theatro positis cum suis armamentis saepe pecunia mutua quae a magistratibus petebatur data actum publicum iuverit\n
EDCS-08600487.translation: The most holy order [grants] to Quintus Avilius Sennius Cominianus, son of Quintus, of the Palatina tribe, in honor of his father Quintus Avilius Hyacinthus, because he, besides his voluntary gifts of spectacles, always assisted the public treasury by providing new sails at his own expense for the theatre, and often by lending money when requested by the magistrates.
EDCS-08600487.summary: A decree honoring Quintus Avilius Sennius Cominianus for his father's financial contributions to public spectacles and the theatre, likely from Apt, 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08600488.text_edition: \n Indelviae Titi filiae Valerillae flaminicae perpetuae quae pro eo honore statuam argenteam cum basi ex HS L milibus nummum in basilica posuit ob quam munificentiam ordo sanctissimus statuam ei ponendam de publico decrevit quae honore contenta inpendium remisit\n
EDCS-08600488.translation: To Indelvia Valerilla, daughter of Titus, perpetual flaminica, who in return for that honor placed a silver statue with a base costing 50,000 sesterces in the basilica, and on account of this generosity the most holy order decreed that a statue be erected to her at public expense, she, content with the honor, remitted the expense.
EDCS-08600488.summary: A decree honoring Indelvia Valerilla, perpetual flaminica, for commissioning a silver statue and for her subsequent remission of public expense, likely from Apt, 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08600489.text_edition: \n Diis Manibus Luci Allidii Postumiani Satrieni Fabati Lucius Allidius Satrienus Nymphodotus libertus\n
EDCS-08600489.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Allidius Satrienus Nymphodotus, freedman, [erected this] to Lucius Allidius Postumianus Satrienus Fabatus.
EDCS-08600489.summary: A funerary dedication by a freedman to his patron, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08600490.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Corneliae Philaenidis et Aemiliae Cornelianae IIIIIIvir Augustalis Lucius Aemilius Asyncritus uxori karissimae et filiae piissimae Lucius Aemilius Honoratus et Lucius Aemilius Cornelianus matri optimae sorori karissimae\n
EDCS-08600490.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Aemilius Honoratus and Lucius Aemilius Cornelianus [erected this] to Cornelia Philaenis and Aemilia Corneliana, wife of the Augustalis and most pious daughter, [as] to their most excellent mother and dearest sister.
EDCS-08600490.summary: A funerary dedication by two sons to their mother and sister, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08600491.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antoni Secundi Vassedonis quaestoris coloniae Aemilia Nigellionis filia Nigrina uxor sibi et viro viva fecit\n
EDCS-08600491.translation: To the departed spirits. Nigrina, daughter of Vassedon, quaestor of the colony, and wife of Antonius Secundus, erected this to herself and her husband while alive.
EDCS-08600491.summary: A funerary dedication set up by a woman for herself and her husband, the quaestor of the colony, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-08600494.summary: A dedication or tombstone for Gaius Veratius Venos Pius, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600495.text_edition: \n Limetio et Quartio Ateponis filii Mercurio et Maiae vota voverunt solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-08600495.translation: To Mercurius and Maia, Limetius and Quartius, sons of Atepo, made and willingly fulfilled their vows, deservedly.
EDCS-08600495.summary: A votive offering to the gods Mercurius and Maia by two individuals, likely from Roman Gaul or Hispania, 1st-3rd century CE.

EDCS-08600719.text_edition: \n IIII et Vario o et Variae I Publius Varius\n
EDCS-08600719.translation: To Publius Varius, for the fourth and fifth days, and to Varia.
EDCS-08600719.summary: This is a dedication to Publius Varius and Varia, possibly related to a specific occasion or festival date, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600720.text_edition: \n Valerius EniValerius Luci filius Anniensi EpaGenuensis\n
EDCS-08600720.translation: Valerius, son of Eni(us) Valerius, from the tribe Aniensis, Epa(froditus?), a native of Genu(m).
EDCS-08600720.summary: This is a funerary or dedication inscription identifying Valerius, his father, his tribe, and his origin, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600722.text_edition: \n Valerius Surus Valerius Maximus Iuliae Iovintinae\n
EDCS-08600722.translation: Valerius Surus [dedicates this?] to Valerius Maximus [and?] Iulia Iovintina.
EDCS-08600722.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or memorial, possibly funerary, from Valerius Surus to Valerius Maximus and Iulia Iovintina, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600729.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a dedication or funerary marker for the daughter of someone named Vebr(ius?), Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600743.text_edition: \n Isuciae Cai filiae A Decimus Valerius Paculus? mil(es)? leg(ionis) XIIII geminae victricis \n
EDCS-08600743.translation: To Isucia, daughter of Caius, by Decimus Valerius Paculus, soldier of the Fourteenth Legion Gemina Victrix.
EDCS-08600743.summary: This inscription is a dedication by Decimus Valerius Paculus, a soldier of the Fourteenth Legion Gemina Victrix, to his daughter Isucia, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600744.summary: This inscription lists the names of three freedmen, possibly on a tombstone or dedication, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600761.text_edition: \n Luci Aetei Aeteius et NV DIN\n
EDCS-08600761.translation: Lucius Aeteius, Aeteius, and NV DIN [names or dedication].
EDCS-08600761.summary: This fragmented inscription seems to name individuals, possibly Lucius Aeteius and another Aeteius, along with further text, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600763.translation: Born for the good of the republic.
EDCS-08600763.summary: This is a phrase likely found on a monument or tomb, praising an individual for their service or virtue related to the state, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08900472.text_edition: \n Caio V Severo filio Mia Marci filia ONIISSIO Numeriae Tessleae Postumiae Quartae\n
EDCS-08900472.translation: To Gaius V. Severus, son of Marcus, [and?] Mia, daughter of ONIISSIO(?), [for?] Numeria Tesslea, Postumia Quarta.
EDCS-08900472.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to be a complex dedication or funerary text involving multiple individuals and relationships, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08901044.text_edition: \n Marci Tucci Luci filii Tromentina Galeonis\n
EDCS-08901044.translation: Of Marcus Tuccius, son of Lucius, of the tribe Tromentina, Galeo.
EDCS-08901044.summary: This inscription records the name and tribal affiliation of Marcus Tuccius, son of Lucius, likely from a tombstone or dedication, Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09001510.text_edition: \n Quomodo hoc plumbum non paret et decadet sic decadat aetas membra vita bos grano mer eorum qui mihi dolum malum fecerunt idem Asuetemeos Secundina quae illum tulit et Verres Tearus et Amarantis et hoc omnia vobis Dii interdico in omnibus sortebus sic tam celebrare Masitlatida concinere Necracantum Colscantum et omnes deos ta datus \n
EDCS-09001510.translation: Just as this lead may fail and decay, so may the lifespan, limbs, and life of those who have plotted evil against me perish; and also that of Asuetemeos Secundina who brought him, and Verres Tearus, and Amarantis; and I forbid all these to you, Gods, in all sorts of ways to celebrate Masitlatida, to sing together Necracantum and Colscantum, and all the gods, it is given.
EDCS-09001510.summary: This is a curse tablet, likely from Roman Britain, where the writer invokes the gods to inflict harm on those who wronged them.

EDCS-09001525.text_edition: \n Ex praediis Agathyrsi Augustae liberti Quintus Pomponius Ianuarius Paetino et Aproniano consulibus\n
EDCS-09001525.translation: From the estates of Agathyrsus, freedman of Augusta, Quintus Pomponius Ianuarius, in the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-09001525.summary: This is a legal or administrative note recording property belonging to Agathyrsus, a freedman of Augusta, dated by the consuls Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-09200341.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus IIIIIIviri Augustalis Luci Aemilii Asyncriti Lucius Aemilius Honoratus et Lucius Aemilius Cornelianus patri optimo\n
EDCS-09200341.translation: To the departed spirits. Lucius Aemilius Honoratus and Lucius Aemilius Cornelianus to their most excellent father, Lucius Aemilius Asynchronous, one of the Six Augustan priests.
EDCS-09200341.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Lucius Aemilius Asynchronous by his sons, who identify him as a member of the Six Augustan priests.

EDCS-09200342.translation: To the departed spirits of Marcus Aetronius Onesimus.
EDCS-09200342.summary: A simple funerary epitaph commemorating Marcus Aetronius Onesimus.

EDCS-09200349.translation: Lucius Cornelius Liber, sacred offering to Apollo.
EDCS-09200349.summary: This inscription records a sacred offering made by Lucius Cornelius Liber to the god Apollo.

EDCS-09200590.translation: To the departed spirits. Publius Licinius Floridus to his dearest wife, Caunia Heortes.
EDCS-09200590.summary: A funerary dedication from Publius Licinius Floridus to his wife, Caunia Heortes.

EDCS-09200591.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the other immortal gods and goddesses, for the safety of the Emperors Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus...
EDCS-09200591.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter and other gods for the well-being of the emperors Lucius Septimius Severus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, likely from the Severan period.

EDCS-09200592.translation: To Sextus Atilius Bellus, son of Aulus, of the Voltinia tribe, according to his will.
EDCS-09200592.summary: This inscription records a dedication or memorial erected in accordance with the will of Sextus Atilius Bellus.

EDCS-09200607.translation: Sextus Geminius Cupitus, sacred offering to Mercury Augustus Artaius, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-09200607.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Sextus Geminius Cupitus to the deity Mercury Augustus Artaius.

EDCS-09200608.translation: Caius Atisius Sedulus, sacred offering to Mercury, in fulfillment of a vow.
EDCS-09200608.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Caius Atisius Sedulus to the god Mercury.

EDCS-09200610.translation: To Quirinus Augustus, Coius Modestus and Julius Macrinus erected (this monument) in response (to a divine command).
EDCS-09200610.summary: This is a dedication to the emperor Quirinus (likely Caracalla) by two individuals, Coius Modestus and Julius Macrinus, likely during the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09200611.translation: To Licinia Macrinula, daughter of Publius, an excellent wife, and to Atisia Macrina, daughter of Gaius, aged 8. Gaius Atisius Paullinus (erected this).
EDCS-09200611.summary: This is a tombstone for Licinia Macrinula and her daughter Atisia Macrina, set up by her husband Gaius Atisius Paullinus, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09200613.translation: To the memory of Titus Aelius Luconianus, Titus Aelius Norbanus set this up for his most dutiful son and for his descendants.
EDCS-09200613.summary: This is a funerary monument dedicated by Titus Aelius Norbanus to his son Titus Aelius Luconianus and for future descendants, likely in the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09200614.translation: Successa willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Minerva.
EDCS-09200614.summary: This is a votive dedication to the goddess Minerva by Successa, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09200615.translation: Gaius Contessius Laevinus, son (of?), a second-rank magistrate with judicial authority, Gaius (name of dedicator?), ordered this to be made in his will.
EDCS-09200615.summary: This inscription records a posthumous dedication by will for Gaius Contessius Laevinus, who held a local magistracy, likely from the late Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-09200616.translation: Quintus Castricius Hermes, a client, (honors?) Quintus Contessius Laevinus, son of Lucius, a second-rank magistrate with judicial authority.
EDCS-09200616.summary: This is an honorary inscription for Quintus Contessius Laevinus, a local magistrate, set up by his client Quintus Castricius Hermes, likely from the late Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-09200630.translation: Lucius Divius Rufus willingly and deservedly fulfilled (his vow) to Mercury Augustus.
EDCS-09200630.summary: This is a votive dedication to Mercury Augustus by Lucius Divius Rufus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09200634.translation: As they had vowed, the brothers Iulus Mansuetus and Karus willingly and deservedly fulfilled (their vow).
EDCS-09200634.summary: This is a votive dedication by the brothers Iulus Mansuetus and Karus, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-09200636.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Claudius, the Pious, Fortunate, Invictus, Augustus, Germanicus Maximus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul, father of the fatherland, proconsul, the detachments and cavalry, and also the officers and standard-bearers, the protectores, proceeding to the province of Narbonensis, under the supervision of Iulius Placidianus, a most perfect man, prefect of the vigiles, devoted to his divine majesty and authority.
EDCS-09200636.summary: This is an extensive military dedication to Emperor Claudius Gothicus, likely from around 270 CE, honoring his troops and their commanders on their way to the Narbonese province.

EDCS-09200638.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Titus Camulus Lavenus, son of Lucius, a veteran of the Legio III Gallica, granted an honorable discharge by the Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius and by the will of the Emperor Hadrianus Augustus, honored with torcs and gold armbands by the vote of the legion. His sister Camulia and Paregoria (set this up) because of his merits, as their most excellent and dutiful patron.
EDCS-09200638.summary: This is a funerary monument for Titus Camulus Lavenus, a veteran of Legio III Gallica, honored by his legion and his family, likely dating to the Hadrianic or Antonine period.

EDCS-09200639.translation: To Decimus Decmanius Capro, sub-prefect of cavalry of the Agrippian ala, who left 50,000 sesterces in his will for statues and bronze [statues?].
EDCS-09200639.summary: This inscription records a posthumous bequest by Decimus Decmanius Capro, a military officer, for the erection of statues.

EDCS-09200641.translation: To Sextus Sammio Severus of the Voltinian tribe, centurion of the I Germanica legion, who was made aquilifer in the same year he began his service; he served as aquilifer for 13 years and was made centurion under the consulship of Gaius Antistius Vetus and Marcus Suillius Nerullinus, by will.
EDCS-09200641.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Sextus Sammio Severus, detailing his military career progression from aquilifer to centurion in the I Germanica legion.

EDCS-09200642.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sextus Attius Atticus, who had held all the offices, [erected this] for Sextus Attius Atticus, his son.
EDCS-09200642.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated by Sextus Attius Atticus to his son, also named Sextus Attius Atticus, who had completed all civic offices.

EDCS-09200643.translation: To Aulus Caprilus Antullus, flamen of Mars. Primulus, his freedman, [erected this] for his patron.
EDCS-09200643.summary: This dedication is from a freedman named Primulus to his patron, Aulus Caprilus Antullus, who served as a flamen Martis.

EDCS-09200644.translation: Publius Cassius Hermetius, duovir Augustalis, set this up for himself while alive.
EDCS-09200644.summary: This is a self-dedicated monument by Publius Cassius Hermetius, who held the office of duovir Augustalis.

EDCS-09200645.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Titus Cassius Mansuetus, flamen of youth, scribe of the military treasury, duovir for administering justice. Cassia Attia, his cousin, [erected this].
EDCS-09200645.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Titus Cassius Mansuetus, who held various civic and religious offices, by his cousin Cassia Attia.

EDCS-09200647.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caius Coelius Lucinus, decurion of the Viennenses. Sextia Threpte, his wife, and his co-heirs, [erected this] for her dearest husband.
EDCS-09200647.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Caius Coelius Lucinus, a decurion of the Viennenses, by his wife and co-heirs.

EDCS-09200648.translation: To Devillia Attica, flaminica. Her heirs [erected this].
EDCS-09200648.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Devillia Attica, a priestess (flaminica), erected by her heirs.

EDCS-09200649.translation: To Gratia Gratina, sweetest soul. Quintus Julius Saturninus, duovir Augustalis, ordered this to be made by will for himself and [for her?].
EDCS-09200649.summary: This inscription, likely funerary, records Gratia Gratina and Quintus Julius Saturninus, a duovir Augustalis, who arranged for its creation by will.

EDCS-09200650.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Publius Helvius Masso, decurion of the Viennenses, [set this up] for himself while alive.
EDCS-09200650.summary: This is a self-dedicated funerary monument by Publius Helvius Masso, a decurion of the Viennenses.

EDCS-09200651.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Sextus Attius Myrosies, minister of the imperial cult and quaestor, to his dearest wife Hilaria Quintilla, priestess, who lived with him from her virginity for 35 years, 2 months, and 11 days, and he made this for himself while alive and dedicated it under the axe.
EDCS-09200651.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a widower for his wife, also including himself, and records her services and their married life.

EDCS-09200652.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Sex. Iulius Condianus, deceased, aged 25, priest of the youth, quaestor of the colony of Vienna, aedile. Marcus Valerius Iulianus, father-in-law, and Valeria Secundilla, to their most devoted husband/son-in-law.
EDCS-09200652.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young man who held several civic offices, set up by his father-in-law and mother-in-law.

EDCS-09200653.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Gaius Papius Secundus, decurion of the colony of Vienna, who died at 40 years of age, and for his son Secundanus, taken away at 10 years of age. Senia Mapcula dedicated this to her dearest husband and son under the axe.
EDCS-09200653.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife and mother for her husband and son, who both died relatively young, likely during the same event.

EDCS-09200654.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Attia Attici filia Bellica to her most devoted husband Sextilius Gallus, minister of the imperial cult, and to herself while alive, and to her descendants, she made this.
EDCS-09200654.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife for her husband, who was a local magistrate, and also for herself and her descendants.

EDCS-09200655.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Quintus Vettius Nobilis, freedman of Epictetus, minister of the imperial cult. His heirs.
EDCS-09200655.summary: This is a funerary epitaph indicating that the heirs of a freedman, who was a local magistrate, erected the monument.

EDCS-09200657.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Materna to her dearest husband Fronton, actor of this place, and her most loving son Philusa, and to her best father Eudrepites, she took care to have this made and dedicated under the axe.
EDCS-09200657.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a wife and son for their husband and father, who was an actor, and also includes a dedication to the wife's father.

EDCS-09200660.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Caius Sollius Marculus, a tax collector of the 40% tax on Gaul, stationed at Cularo, who died at 26 years of age. Caius Sollius Marcus, father, to his most devoted son, and Attia Marciana and Marcula, sisters, to their most devoted brother, and Attia Aurelia, wife, to her incomparable husband, dedicated this under the axe.
EDCS-09200660.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for a young man who worked as a tax collector, with dedications from his father, sisters, and wife.

EDCS-09200671.translation: To the spirits of the departed, for the deceased aged 26. Bitugia Modestina, mother.
EDCS-09200671.summary: This is a funerary epitaph set up by a mother for her deceased daughter.

EDCS-09200677.translation: To Perpetual Eternity. For Lucius Devillius Lucinus, father, and Lucius Devillius Martian[us], brother.
EDCS-09200677.summary: This is a dedication to family members, likely on a tomb or memorial, possibly honoring those who have passed on.

EDCS-09200682.translation: To the spirits of the departed. For Iulia Martia, whom Marcus Iulius Saturninus, the heir, ordered to be made by her will, and Titus Andius Eu[...]
EDCS-09200682.summary: This is a funerary epitaph indicating that a will mandated the construction of the monument for Iulia Martia, with an incomplete inscription for another individual.

EDCS-09200683.translation: To the departed spirits. Vettius Tertiolus to his incomparable wife, Iulia Severa.
EDCS-09200683.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Iulia Severa, dedicated by her husband Vettius Tertiolus.

EDCS-09200684.translation: To Tertia and Sabina, daughters of Iulia [---]mari and Titus.
EDCS-09200684.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary epitaph mentioning two daughters, Tertia and Sabina, and their mother Iulia [---]mari.

EDCS-09200685.text_edition: \n Lucio Iuventio Victoris et Paulinulae filio Cassiano Bimo Iuventius Victor\n
EDCS-09200685.translation: To Cassianus Bimus, son of Lucius Iuventius Victor and Paulinula. Iuventius Victor [dedicated this].
EDCS-09200685.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication of a tomb by Iuventius Victor to his son Cassianus Bimus.

EDCS-09200687.translation: [---] and Larinia erected [this] at their own expense.
EDCS-09200687.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the erection of a monument at private expense by Larinius and Larinia.

EDCS-09200688.translation: To the departed spirits and eternal rest. Publius Devillius Lucinus to his most excellent and most holy wife, Lutatia Liciniana, daughter of Macedonian(a); and Devillius Lutatius and Lucina to their most beloved mother.
EDCS-09200688.summary: This is a complex funerary epitaph for Lutatia Liciniana, dedicated by her husband Publius Devillius Lucinus and her children Devillius Lutatius and Lucina.

EDCS-09200689.translation: To the departed spirits. Magi Macrinus and Atilius [erected this] to their most devoted father, Marcus Magius Potentinus.
EDCS-09200689.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication by sons Magi Macrinus and Atilius to their father Marcus Magius Potentinus.

EDCS-09200690.translation: To the departed spirits. Cn(aeus) Verg() Zosimus(?) to his most holy wife, Magia Rufina.
EDCS-09200690.summary: This funerary epitaph is for Magia Rufina, dedicated by her husband Cn(aeus) Verg() Zosimus(?).

EDCS-09200691.translation: [---] to perpetual rest. Aelia Italica and Aelius Taurinianus erected this at their own expense to their most devoted parents, Nammia Saturnina and Taurus.
EDCS-09200691.summary: This funerary epitaph records the dedication by Aelia Italica and Aelius Taurinianus at their own expense to their parents Nammia Saturnina and Taurus.

EDCS-09200692.translation: To the departed spirits. Titus Valerius Valerianus to his most holy wife, Nigidia Iuliana.
EDCS-09200692.summary: This funerary epitaph is for Nigidia Iuliana, dedicated by her husband Titus Valerius Valerianus.

EDCS-09200693.translation: Pompeia Severa, daughter of Lucius, ordered this to be set up in her will.
EDCS-09200693.summary: This funerary epitaph states that Pompeia Severa, daughter of Lucius, commissioned this monument in her will.

EDCS-09200694.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Vassillus and Valeria [set this up] for their parents, Lucius Primus Valerius and Pottia Carilla, and Primus.
EDCS-09200694.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication to the underworld spirits by Vassillus and Valeria for their parents.

EDCS-09200696.translation: [To the spirits of the underworld]. Vitalis, son of Quintus Quintius Valentinus, deceased, aged 23. Bitunia Titiola [set this up].
EDCS-09200696.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Vitalis, son of Quintus Quintius Valentinus, at the age of 23.

EDCS-09200697.translation: To Quintus Scribonius Lucullus [of the Voltinia tribe] and to Julia Gratilla, his wife, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-09200697.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Quintus Scribonius Lucullus and his wife, Julia Gratilla.

EDCS-09200698.translation: To Marcus Titius Gratus, son of Marcus, of the Voltinia tribe.
EDCS-09200698.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Marcus Titius Gratus.

EDCS-09200699.translation: [To the spirits of the underworld]. Sextus Titius Pacatus, son of [---], of the Voltinia tribe, [set this up] for himself and his father.
EDCS-09200699.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication set up by Sextus Titius Pacatus for himself and his father.

EDCS-09200700.translation: To Gaius Titius (?) Titulus, son of Vatio (?), of the Voltinia (?) tribe.
EDCS-09200700.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Gaius Titius Titulus.

EDCS-09200705.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Veratia Lucina, daughter of Lucius.
EDCS-09200705.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication to Veratia Lucina.

EDCS-09200706.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Sextus Vinicius Iulianus, deceased in Rome. Vinicia Vera [set this up] for her most devoted father.
EDCS-09200706.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Vinicia Vera to her father, Sextus Vinicius Iulianus.

EDCS-09200707.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. For Uritea Tita. Publius Cassius Hermetius [set this up] for his most holy wife.
EDCS-09200707.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication by Publius Cassius Hermetius to his wife, Uritea Tita.

EDCS-09200708.translation: [To the spirits of the underworld]. For [his] most holy sister.
EDCS-09200708.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication to an unnamed sister.

EDCS-09200709.translation: Grieving his mother's fall, her son was saddened.
EDCS-09200709.summary: This is a funerary epitaph where a son expresses his grief over his mother's death.

EDCS-09200743.translation: To Gaius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, Emperor, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power, consul. The ratiarii from Volubilis.
EDCS-09200743.summary: This is a dedication to the emperor Gaius Caesar (Caligula) by the ratiarii (military equipment repairmen or possibly a group of soldiers) from Volubilis, likely dating to his reign (37-41 CE).

EDCS-09200745.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucio Iulio Frontonis filio Voltinia Iuliano IIviro aerarii Iul<l=I>us et Iucundus patri et Vireia Secundinula marito \n
