EDCS-09500365.translation: To the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th, 21 miles.
EDCS-09500365.summary: This is an inscription marking a road of 21 miles dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-09500366.translation: To the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th, 22 miles.
EDCS-09500366.summary: This is an inscription marking a road of 22 miles dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-09500368.translation: To the Emperor Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th, [---] [6 characters illegible].
EDCS-09500368.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely marking a road of unspecified length.

EDCS-09500371.translation: [To the Emperor Titus] / [Aelius Hadrianus] / [Antoninus] / Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 7th time, consul for the 4th, 30 miles.
EDCS-09500371.summary: This is a restored inscription marking a road of 30 miles dedicated to Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-09701056.summary: This is a very brief inscription dedicating something to the deified Emperor Aurelian.

EDCS-09701066.text_edition: \n Marti Augusto Lucius Accius Lemnus votum solvit\n
EDCS-09701066.translation: Lucius Accius Lemnus fulfilled his vow to Mars Augustus.
EDCS-09701066.summary: This inscription records Lucius Accius Lemnus fulfilling a vow to the god Mars Augustus.

EDCS-09800229.text_edition: \n Publio Licinio Marci filio Macrino Lucius Licinius Luci filius Blaesus patruo\n
EDCS-09800229.translation: Lucius Licinius Blaesus, son of Lucius, from his paternal uncle, to Publius Licinius Macrinus, son of Marcus.
EDCS-09800229.summary: This is a dedication from Lucius Licinius Blaesus to his paternal uncle, Publius Licinius Macrinus.

EDCS-09800234.text_edition: \n Avete Sextus Parredius Sexti libertus Arruntio haruspex pedes quadrati XI\n
EDCS-09800234.translation: Greetings. Sextus Parredius, son of Sextus, freedman of Arruntius, the haruspex, [offers] 11 square feet.
EDCS-09800234.summary: This inscription records Sextus Parredius, a freedman and haruspex, dedicating an area of 11 square feet.

EDCS-09800475.translation: Tiberius Caesar, son of the deified Augustus, Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 33rd time, repaired and restored [this].
EDCS-09800475.summary: This inscription states that Emperor Tiberius repaired and restored something, noting his tribunician power for the 33rd time.

EDCS-09800476.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul designate for the 2nd time, imperator for the 2nd time, repaired [this].
EDCS-09800476.summary: This inscription records that Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus repaired something, noting his consular and imperatorial titles.

EDCS-09800479.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul-designate for the second time, imperator for the second time, rebuilt [this].
EDCS-09800479.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a structure by the emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, likely during his second consulship.

EDCS-09800481.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 33rd time, rebuilt and restored [this].
EDCS-09800481.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding and restoration of a structure by Emperor Tiberius, specifically noting his 33rd tribunician power.

EDCS-09800483.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 33rd [year], rebuilt and restored [this] in the 13th year [of his reign/power?].
EDCS-09800483.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding and restoration of a structure by Emperor Tiberius, noting his 33rd tribunician power and possibly a 13th year marker.

EDCS-09800485.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland, restored [this] in the 13th year [of his reign/power?].
EDCS-09800485.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a structure by Emperor Antoninus Pius, noting his eighth tribunician power, fourth consulship, and a 13th year marker.

EDCS-09800492.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul-designate for the second time, imperator for the second time, rebuilt [this].
EDCS-09800492.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a structure by the emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, likely during his second consulship.

EDCS-09800493.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul-designate for the second time, imperator for the second time, rebuilt [this].
EDCS-09800493.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a structure by the emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, likely during his second consulship.

EDCS-09800494.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 33rd time, rebuilt and restored [this] in the 7th year [of his reign/power?].
EDCS-09800494.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding and restoration of a structure by Emperor Tiberius, noting his 33rd tribunician power and a 7th year marker.

EDCS-09800495.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland, restored [this] in the 7th year [of his reign/power?].
EDCS-09800495.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a structure by Emperor Antoninus Pius, noting his eighth tribunician power, fourth consulship, and a 7th year marker.

EDCS-09800497.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, consul-designate for the second time, imperator for the second time, rebuilt [this].
EDCS-09800497.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding of a structure by the emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, likely during his second consulship.

EDCS-09800498.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Hadrian, Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 8th time, imperator for the second time, consul for the fourth time, father of the fatherland, restored [this] in the 6th year [of his reign/power?].
EDCS-09800498.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a structure by Emperor Antoninus Pius, noting his eighth tribunician power, fourth consulship, and a 6th year marker.

EDCS-09800499.translation: Emperor Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the divine Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 33rd time, repaired and restored this [structure].
EDCS-09800499.summary: This inscription records the repair and restoration of a structure by Emperor Tiberius, indicated by his titles and 33rd year of tribunician power.

EDCS-09800500.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine [Augustus], chief priest, consul for the 12th time, designated consul for the 13th time, imperator for the 14th time, holding tribunician power for the 20th time.
EDCS-09800500.summary: This inscription lists the titles and honors of Emperor Augustus, including his consular and tribunician powers, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-09800501.translation: Emperor Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the divine Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for the 33rd time, repaired and restored this [structure].

EDCS-09800516.translation: Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 8th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of his country, restored [this].

EDCS-09800521.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of a god, chief priest, consul for the 12th time, consul designate for the 13th time, imperator for the 14th time, holder of tribunician power for the 20th time.
EDCS-09800521.summary: This inscription lists the titles and powers of an emperor, likely from the Julio-Claudian or Flavian period, indicating significant accumulated honors.

EDCS-09800522.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul designate for the 2nd time, imperator for the 2nd time, repaired [this].

EDCS-09800525.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul designate for the 2nd time, imperator for the 2nd time, repaired [this] (in its) 85th year.
EDCS-09800525.summary: This inscription records repairs made by Emperor Claudius, detailing his titles and powers, and the age of the monument, likely from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-09800526.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power, consul designate for the 2nd time, imperator for the 2nd time, repaired [this].
EDCS-09800526.summary: This inscription records repairs made by Emperor Claudius, detailing his titles and powers, likely from the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-09800528.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine, pontifex maximus, consul for the 12th time, consul-elect for the 13th time, imperator for the 14th time, with tribunician power for the 20th time.
EDCS-09800528.summary: This inscription records the titles and offices held by Emperor Augustus, likely on a public building or monument, dating to his 20th year of tribunician power.

EDCS-09800536.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul-elect for the 2nd time, imperator for the 2nd time, rebuilt this.

EDCS-09800537.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a building by Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, dating to his second consulship and second acclamation as imperator.

EDCS-09800538.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul-elect for the 2nd time, imperator for the 2nd time, rebuilt this.
EDCS-09800538.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a building by Emperor Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, dating to his second consulship and second acclamation as imperator.

EDCS-09800539.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the divine Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 33rd time, rebuilt and restored this (building), aged 73.
EDCS-09800539.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding and restoration of a structure by Emperor Tiberius, noting his tribunician power and his age, likely around 37 CE.

EDCS-09800540.translation: Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the divine, pontifex maximus, consul for the 12th time, consul-elect for the 13th time, imperator for the 14th time, with tribunician power for the 20th time.
EDCS-09800540.summary: This inscription records the titles and offices held by Emperor Augustus, likely on a public building or monument, dating to his 20th year of tribunician power.

EDCS-09800544.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the divine Augustus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 33rd time, rebuilt and restored this (building), aged 69.

EDCS-09800545.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, son of Drusus, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power, consul-elect for the 2nd time, imperator for the 2nd time, rebuilt this.

EDCS-09800549.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 33rd time, repaired and restored it [the building/structure]; 63 [paces/units].

EDCS-09800551.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 33rd time, repaired and restored it [the building/structure]; 62 [paces/units].

EDCS-09800558.translation: Tiberius Caesar Augustus, son of the deified Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 33rd time, repaired [the building/structure].
EDCS-09800558.summary: This inscription records the repair of a structure by Emperor Tiberius, dating to his 33rd year of tribunician power.

EDCS-09800559.translation: To our lord Gaius Pius Esuvius Tetricus, most noble Caesar, son of Emperor Gaius Pius Esuvius Tetricus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 2nd time, consul.

EDCS-09800560.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus Germanicus, chief priest, greatest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 14th time, father of the country, consul for the 4th time, censor, repaired [the building/structure]; 3 (?) units.
EDCS-09800560.summary: This inscription records the repair of a structure by Emperor Claudius, likely dating to around 47-48 CE.

EDCS-09800564.translation: The Emperor Caesar Augustus, son of the deified [father], father of the country, chief priest, greatest, consul for the 13th time, with tribunician power for the 22nd time, imperator for the 14th time; 16 [paces/units]; Forum Julii 917 [paces/units]; the civitas of the Vasionenses of the Vocontii 898 [paces/units]; the kingdom of the Cotti [?] [lacuna].
EDCS-09800564.summary: This inscription, likely from the reign of Augustus, records distances in paces to Forum Julii and a civitas of the Vocontii, and possibly refers to the regnum of the Cotti.

EDCS-09800569.text_edition: \n Flavio Valerio Constantino nobilissimo Caesari milia p(assuum) XXXIIII\n

EDCS-09800569.summary: This inscription records a distance of 34 miles to Flavius Valerius Constantinus (Constantine the Great), likely a milestone.

EDCS-09800570.translation: To Marcus Numerius Numerianus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth; from Narbo, 1 mile.
EDCS-09800570.summary: This inscription records a distance of 1 mile from Narbo to Marcus Numerius Numerianus, likely a milestone.

EDCS-09800573.translation: To Gaius Pius Tetricus, most noble Caesar, prince of the youth, consul; 29 [units].
EDCS-09800573.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Gaius Pius Tetricus (Tetricus II), likely from the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-09800577.translation: To our Lord Galerius Valerius Maximianus Pius Felix Invictus Semper Augustus Caesar. 15 miles. To our Lord Flavius Julius Dalmatius, most noble Caesar. 15 miles. Emperor Caesar Gaius Julius Verus Maximinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holder of tribunician power for the [X]th time, consul for the second time, father of the country, proconsul, restored [this road]. 15 miles.

EDCS-09800578.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, grandson of Marcus [Aurelius] Valerius Maximinus Augustus, son of the divine Constantius Augustus. 16 miles. To our Lords Magnus Maximus and Flavius Victor. 16 miles. To our Lords Flavius Valentinianus and Flavius Theodosius and Flavius Arcadius, born for the good of the state. 16 miles.
EDCS-09800578.summary: This inscription marks milestones for several emperors, including Constantine, Magnus Maximus, Flavius Victor, Valentinian, Theodosius, and Arcadius, spanning the late 4th century CE.

EDCS-09800584.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Valerius Maximinus Pius [Felix Invictus] Augustus.
EDCS-09800584.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription dedicated to Emperor Maximinus, likely from the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-10502259.translation: [Nero] Claudius, son of the divine Claudius, grandson of Germanicus Caesar, great-grandson of Tiberius Caesar Augustus, great-great-grandson of the divine Augustus, Caesar Augustus Germanicus, Pontifex Maximus.
EDCS-10502259.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription naming Emperor Nero Claudius, likely dating to the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-11700350.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Dionysius Caius Iulius Apolinaris\n
EDCS-11700350.translation: Gaius Julius Dionysius, Gaius Julius Apollinaris.
EDCS-11700350.summary: This is a very brief inscription likely recording the names of two individuals, possibly related or associated.

EDCS-11700356.text_edition: \n Caio Novellio Quinti filio Aniensi veterano legionis VIIII\n
EDCS-11700356.translation: To Gaius Novellius, son of Quintus, of the Aniensis tribe, a veteran of the Ninth Legion.
EDCS-11700356.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Gaius Novellius, a veteran of the Ninth Legion, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11700365.text_edition: \n Sextae Satiae Sexti filiae Maxsumae sic coniugi Quintus Masonius Kari libertus Alco\n

EDCS-11700365.summary: This inscription is a dedication from Quintus Masonius Alco to his wife Sextae Satias, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11700387.text_edition: \n Caius Cornelius Carpophorus sibi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-11700387.translation: Gaius Cornelius Carpophorus made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-11700387.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Gaius Cornelius Carpophorus commissioned his own monument during his lifetime, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11700430.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Tullius [dedicated this].
EDCS-11700430.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, likely made by an individual named Tullius.

EDCS-11700434.text_edition: \n Reiuniis Marcus Iulius Iustus hastatus primus legionis IIII Macedonicae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11700434.translation: Marcus Julius Justus, hastatus primus of the Fourth Macedonian Legion, freed from vows willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-11700434.summary: This inscription records Marcus Julius Justus, a soldier of the Fourth Macedonian Legion, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11700436.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the departed spirits.

EDCS-11700437.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Iulio Grato Iulia Grata patri pientissimo viva fecit\n
EDCS-11700437.translation: To the departed spirits. Iulia Grata made this for her most dutiful father, Marcus Iulius Gratus, while she was alive.
EDCS-11700437.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Iulia Grata for her father, Marcus Iulius Gratus.

EDCS-11700439.text_edition: \n ------ A]rausiensis Secundus filio mo vivos sic fecit\n
EDCS-11700439.translation: Secundus of Arausio made this for his sons while he was alive.
EDCS-11700439.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Secundus of Arausio for his sons.

EDCS-11700454.translation: Under Gaius Bruttius Crispinus and Lucius Roscio Aelianus, consuls, on the tenth day before the Kalends of March. By decree of the decurions of the municipium of Dinium and Bodionticum, having been summoned to the curia, the scribes who wrote this decree were: Marcus Messius Serenus, flamen of the civitas; Gaius Beiuclus Sequens; Lucius Maculius Severus; Gaius Valerius Victor; Marcus Marini. And Valerius Licinianus, Marcus [---], duumviri, [---] and quaestors, it was decided that [---] should be provided by them and those [---]. Titus Veranius L[---]nia because [---] Veranius [---] justice [---] it is lawful [---] Sextus Sa[---] Satu[---] qua[---]
EDCS-11700454.summary: This inscription records a municipal decree from Dinium and Bodionticum, detailing financial matters and appointments of officials, dated to February 21st under consuls Gaius Bruttius Crispinus and Lucius Roscio Aelianus.

EDCS-11700464.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cnaei Licini Marci Licinia Veneria marito incomparabili et dendrophori Aquenses\n
EDCS-11700464.translation: To the departed spirits. Licinia Veneria and the dendrophori of Aquae for their incomparable husband, Cnaeus Licinius Marcu.
EDCS-11700464.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Licinia Veneria and the dendrophori of Aquae for Cnaeus Licinius Marcu.

EDCS-11700510.text_edition: \n Fausta Herculi votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-11700510.translation: Fausta willingly and deservedly paid her vow to Hercules.
EDCS-11700510.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Fausta fulfilling a vow to Hercules.

EDCS-11700567.text_edition: \n Lucio Antonio Rufino IIviro Thallus et Rythmus liberti\n
EDCS-11700567.translation: Thallus and Rythmus, freedmen, for Lucius Antonius Rufus, duumvir.
EDCS-11700567.summary: This is a dedication by the freedmen Thallus and Rythmus for Lucius Antonius Rufus, a duumvir.

EDCS-11700768.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Attius Soficus Aurelio Victorino\n
EDCS-11700768.translation: To the departed spirits. Attius Soficus for Aurelius Victorinus.
EDCS-11700768.summary: This is a funerary dedication by Attius Soficus for Aurelius Victorinus.

EDCS-11700772.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Sextus Iulius Saturninus.

EDCS-11800691.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus coniiuxs sic Aniccellus Maxsentio sic possuit sic aggenti sic titulum\n
EDCS-11800691.translation: To the departed spirits. The wife set this up for her husband. Aniccellus set it up, he acted, he erected the tombstone.
EDCS-11800691.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a wife for her husband, Aniccellus.

EDCS-11800696.text_edition: \n Aproniano et Paetino consulibus Pomponius Vitalis ex praediis Anni Veri Quintanensibus\n
EDCS-11800696.translation: In the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus, Pomponius Vitalis from the estates of Anna Vera at Quintanenses.
EDCS-11800696.summary: This is a dedication concerning property and its owner, dated to the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-11801012.text_edition: \n Temperant Temperant Vis tubam ITM alta palma tradit LTX acclamabit Madit aram Andromedam raptam\n
EDCS-11801012.translation: Temperant Temperant gives the trumpet from the high palm tree; Temperant will shout aloud; Madit gives the altar of Andromeda, snatched away.
EDCS-11801012.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedicatory or commemorative text, possibly related to a performance or ritual, referencing mythological figures and actions.

EDCS-11801017.text_edition: \n Petale Antonia votum solvit libens merito Fortunae\n
EDCS-11801017.translation: Petale Antonia gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to Fortuna.
EDCS-11801017.summary: This is a votive inscription by Petale Antonia to the goddess Fortuna.

EDCS-11801021.text_edition: \n Sextarii XXXVI Sisenna Statilio consule Graviscanum\n
EDCS-11801021.translation: 36 sextarii in the consulship of Sisenna and Graviscanus.
EDCS-11801021.summary: This inscription records a quantity of 36 sextarii, possibly of an unspecified commodity or payment, dated to the consulship of Sisenna and Graviscanus.

EDCS-11801022.text_edition: \n Caio Asinio Pollione consule I Acini Phileros\n
EDCS-11801022.translation: In the consulship of Gaius Asinius Pollio, Acinius Phileros.
EDCS-11801022.summary: This is a dedication or record of an event during the consulship of Gaius Asinius Pollio, attributed to Acinius Phileros.

EDCS-11801038.summary: This is a brief funerary or farewell inscription addressed to Pyrame.

EDCS-12100286.text_edition: \n Tito Statilio Tauro Lucio Cassio Longino consulibus X Kalendas Octobres numini Augusti votum susceptum a plebe Narbonensium in perpetuom sic quod bonum faustum felixque sit Imperatori Caesari Divi filio Augusto patri patriae pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXXIIII coniugi liberis gentique eius senatui populoque Romano et colonis incolisque coloniae Iuliae Paternae Narbonis Marti qui se numini eius in perpetuum colendo obligaverunt pleps sic Narbonensium aram Narbone in foro posuit ad quam quot annis VIIII Kalendas Octobres qua die eum saeculi felicitas orbi terrarum rectorem edidit tres equites Romani a plebe et tres libertini hostias singulas inmolent et colonis et incolis ad supplicandum numini eius thus et vinum de suo ea die praestent et VIII Kalendas Octobres thus vinum colonis et incolis item praestent Kalendis quoque Ianuariis thus et vinum colonis et incolis praestent VII quoque Idus Ianuarias qua die primum imperium orbis terrarum auspicatus est thure vino supplicent et hostias singulas inmolent et colonis incolisque thus vinum ea die praestent et pridie Kalendas Iunias quod ea die Tito Statilio Tauro Manio Aemilio Lepido consulibus iudicia plebis decurionibus coniunxit hostias singulas inmolent et thus et vinum ad supplicandum numini eius colonis et incolis praestent exque iis tribus equitibus Romanis tribusve libertinis unu[s ------ Pleps Narbonensis aram numinis Augusti dedicavit [] legibus iis quae infra scriptae sunt numen Caesaris Augusti patris patriae quando tibi hodie hanc aram dabo dedicaboque his legibus hisque regionibus dabo dedicabo quas hic hodie palam dixero uti infimum solum huiusque arae titulorumque est si quis tergere ornare reficere volet quod beneficii causa fiat ius fasque esto sive quis hostia sacrum faxit qui magmentum nec protollat idcirco tamen probe factum esto si quis huic arae donum dare augereque volet liceto eademque lex ei dono esto quae arae est ceterae leges huic arae titulisque eadem sunto quae sunt arae Dianae in Aventino hisce legibus hisque regionibus sicuti dixi hanc tibi aram pro Imperatore Caesare Augusto patre patriae pontifice maximo tribunicia potestate XXXV coniuge liberis genteque eius senatu populoque Romano colonis incolisque coloniae Iuliae Paternae Narbonis Marti qui se numini eius in perpetuum colendo obligaverunt doque dedicoque uti sies volens propitium\n
EDCS-12100286.translation: In the consulship of Titus Statilius Taurus and Lucius Cassius Longinus, on the 8th day before the Kalends of October, a vow undertaken by the people of Narbonensium for eternity to the divine Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 34th time, to his wife, children, family, the Senate, and the Roman People, and to the colonists and inhabitants of the colony of Iulia Paternensis Narbonis Martis who have bound themselves to worship his divinity forever: thus the people of Narbonensium have placed an altar to his divinity in the forum at Narbo, to which every year on the 8th day before the Kalends of October, the day on which the good fortune of the age gave him as ruler to the world, three Roman knights chosen by the people and three freedmen shall each sacrifice one victim, and shall provide from their own means oil and wine on that day for the colonists and inhabitants for supplicating his divinity, and on the 9th day before the Kalends of October, they shall likewise provide oil and wine for the colonists and inhabitants; also on the Kalends of January, they shall provide oil and wine for the colonists and inhabitants; and on the 7th day before the Ides of January, on which day he first began his rule over the world, they shall supplicate with incense and wine and sacrifice one victim each, and shall provide oil and wine for the colonists and inhabitants on that day; and on the day before the Kalends of June, because on that day, in the consulship of Titus Statilius Taurus and Manius Aemilius Lepidus, he joined the judgments of the people with the decurions, they shall sacrifice one victim each, and provide incense and wine for supplicating his divinity for the colonists and inhabitants. And from these, three Roman knights and three freedmen [---]. The People of Narbonensium dedicated the altar to the divinity of Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, according to the laws written below: When I will give and dedicate this altar to you today, O divinity of Caesar Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 35th time, to his wife, children, family, the Senate, and the Roman People, and to the colonists and inhabitants of the colony of Iulia Paternensis Narbonis Martis who have bound themselves to worship his divinity forever, I will give and dedicate it according to these laws and these boundaries which I will declare here today in public, just as the lowest ground of this altar and its inscriptions are: if anyone wishes to clean, adorn, or repair it, let it be lawful and right for it to be done for the sake of kindness. If anyone sacrifices a victim and does not prolong the offering, nevertheless let it be considered properly done. If anyone wishes to give or add to this altar, let it be permitted, and let the same law apply to that gift as to the altar itself. Let the other laws for this altar and its inscriptions be the same as those for the altar of Diana on the Aventine. According to these laws and these boundaries, as I have said, I give and dedicate this altar to you, for Emperor Caesar Augustus, Father of the Fatherland, Pontifex Maximus, with tribunician power for the 35th time, to his wife, children, family, the Senate, and the Roman People, and to the colonists and inhabitants of the colony of Iulia Paternensis Narbonis Martis who have bound themselves to worship his divinity forever, and I give and dedicate it, so that you may be willing and propitious.
EDCS-12100286.summary: This is a lengthy public inscription from Narbo, dated to 11 BCE, detailing the dedication of an altar to the divine Augustus by the local populace and establishing complex rules for its maintenance, sacrifices, and offerings, with specific provisions for Roman knights and freedmen.

EDCS-13301723.text_edition: \n Genio viciniae castellanae Olbiensiu<m=N> Lucius Rupilius Iacchus dono dedit cum suis \n
EDCS-13301723.translation: Lucius Rupilius Iacchus gave this gift to the genius of the Olbiensian castle neighborhood, along with his household.
EDCS-13301723.summary: This is a dedication to the local genius (guardian spirit) of a castle neighborhood in Olbia, made by Lucius Rupilius Iacchus.

EDCS-13301729.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Strenuae Caius Iulius Vitalis patronae cui et matri vivus fecit\n
EDCS-13301729.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld, for Iulia Strenua, Caius Iulius Vitalis, to his patroness, whom he also made for as his mother while alive.
EDCS-13301729.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated by Caius Iulius Vitalis to his patroness, Iulia Strenua, whom he also considered a mother.

EDCS-13301734.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning two individuals, possibly daughters named Amoena, with a partial name preceding.

EDCS-13301743.translation: To Caesar Germanicus, holder of tribunician power and thrice consul, Sextus Aelianus Pisinus, Augustalis, dedicated this from his own funds.
EDCS-13301743.summary: This is a dedication by Sextus Aelianus Pisinus to Caesar Germanicus, likely dating to the period of Germanicus's tribunician power.

EDCS-13301826.translation: Sacred to the departed shades. Caecinia Euporia, who lived 36 years, 3 months, and 23 days, Gallius Marcellinus made for his well-deserving wife.
EDCS-13301826.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caecinia Euporia, dedicated by her husband Gallius Marcellinus.

EDCS-13301860.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly dedicating something to a deified figure, perhaps Claudius.

EDCS-13301884.translation: For the Great Mother of the Gods, for the health of Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Augustus, and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, and [Publius Septimius Geta Caesar], and Julia Augusta, the public community of the Vocontii performed a taurobolium, with Talusius Appianus presiding publicly.
EDCS-13301884.summary: This inscription records a taurobolium, a ritual sacrifice, performed by the public community of the Vocontii for the health of the imperial family, including emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, and empress Julia Domna.

EDCS-13301933.translation: [3]E[3] of Lucius Cominius [3], their sons. Lucius Cominius Paulinus [dedicated this] to his most excellent parents and his most devoted brother.
EDCS-13301933.summary: This is a dedication by Lucius Cominius Paulinus to his parents and brother.

EDCS-13301934.translation: To Decimus Dannius Capito of the Voltinia tribe.
EDCS-13301934.summary: This is a dedication to Decimus Dannius Capito.

EDCS-13302223.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Cluvi Silani Cluvia Sina Cai liberta coniugi kariss<i=E>mo viva posuit \n
EDCS-13302223.translation: To the departed shades. Cluvia Sina, freedwoman of Caius, placed this for her most beloved husband, Caius Cluvius Silanus, while she was alive.
EDCS-13302223.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Caius Cluvius Silanus, dedicated by his wife Cluvia Sina.

EDCS-13302230.translation: Titus Romanius made this while alive.
EDCS-13302230.summary: This inscription records Titus Romanius making something during his lifetime.

EDCS-13302236.translation: [3] and for Publius Iulius, son of Publius, of the Pupinia tribe, [3] a quinquennial official, and for Publius Iulius Firmus, son of Publius, [3] his brother.
EDCS-13302236.summary: This fragmented inscription appears to record dedications or mentions related to members of the Iulius family, including a quinquennial official.

EDCS-13302240.translation: To the departed shades. To Zozimes, freedwoman of Caius, [his?] wife, most beloved.
EDCS-13302240.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Zozimes, possibly the wife of the person who erected the monument.

EDCS-13302249.translation: To Aponius Chereas, son of Lucius, of the Papiria tribe, augur, quaestor of Narbo Martius, honored with the ornaments of an aedile; for which service he endowed the public treasury of Narbo with 150,000 sesterces. Also honored with the ornaments of an aedile, duovir, and flamen, and augur in Sicily, in the Thermis of Syracuse, and in Panormus. Honored with the ornaments of an aedile, duovir, and flamen, and augur. Aponius Blastus, to his excellent patron. A place was granted by decree of the decurions.
EDCS-13302249.summary: This is a dedication honoring Aponius Chereas, a prominent citizen of Narbo, who was also honored for his public services in Sicily, and includes a grant of land by the local senate.

EDCS-13302251.text_edition: \n naviculario Cornelius Panegyricus et Cornelius Chryseros amico optimo\n
EDCS-13302251.translation: Cornelius Panegyricus, a shipowner, and Cornelius Chryseros, to their excellent friend.
EDCS-13302251.summary: This is a funerary dedication from two shipowners to their friend.

EDCS-13302253.translation: Lived: Titus Felix, freedman of Titus, holder of the quaestorship four times, Augustalis.
EDCS-13302253.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Titus Felix, who held multiple civic and religious offices.

EDCS-13302257.translation: Lived: Titus Attius Quartus, stonemason. 15 feet in all directions.
EDCS-13302257.summary: This tombstone marks the burial plot of Titus Attius Quartus, a stonemason, specifying the dimensions of the plot.

EDCS-13302263.text_edition: \n Marco Fabio MMMarcorum liberto GI mercatori Cordubensi \n
EDCS-13302263.translation: To Marcus Fabius, freedman of M. Marcus, merchant of Corduba.
EDCS-13302263.summary: This is a dedication to Marcus Fabius, a freedman and merchant from Corduba.

EDCS-13302265.text_edition: \n Vivus Quintus Flavius Quinti libertus Primus lardarius [] []\n
EDCS-13302265.translation: Alive: Quintus Flavius Primus, freedman of Quintus, pork butcher.
EDCS-13302265.summary: This inscription indicates that Quintus Flavius Primus, a pork butcher, made his own tomb in advance.

EDCS-13302329.translation: To [---], son of Titus, and to his brother [---]. [---] made this for [---] and his brother [---], while alive. [---] made this.
EDCS-13302329.summary: This is a dedication for a living person and their brother, likely for a tomb, with the dedicant identified by patronymics.

EDCS-13302678.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Licinius Surus vivos sic sibi et Quinto Licinio Repentino patrono\n
EDCS-13302678.translation: To the Divine Shades. Quintus Licinius Surus, while alive, to himself and to Quintus Licinius Repentinus, his patron.
EDCS-13302678.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by Quintus Licinius Surus for himself and his patron, Quintus Licinius Repentinus.

EDCS-13500315.summary: This is a dedication to Auribus Loreia Pia, identified as a minister.

EDCS-13800219.text_edition: \n Senatus populusque Romanus Imperatori Caesari Divi filio Augusto consuli VIII dedit clupeum virtutis clementiae iustitiae pietatis erga deos patriamque\n
EDCS-13800219.translation: The Senate and the Roman people gave to the Emperor Caesar, son of the Divine one, Augustus, in his eighth consulship, a shield for his valor, clemency, justice, piety towards the gods, and fatherland.
EDCS-13800219.summary: This inscription records the Roman Senate and people presenting Emperor Augustus with a shield in recognition of his virtues and service.

EDCS-14000029.text_edition: \n SD I VK XXIII insulae Furianae coloniae iugera XX et nova VII praestant aera IV |(denarii) I|(bes) |(semuncia) solvunt heredes Firmi Secundi SD I VK XXII coloniae iugera XCVII|(bes) Quinto Curtio Rufo IIviro et inventore nova iugera XV|(dodrans) praestant aera III |(denarii) III|(dodrans) |(semuncia) TAI solvunt heredes Firmi Secundi\n
EDCS-14000029.translation: The twenty-third cohort of volunteers from the Furian estate of the colony provide twenty jugera of land and seven new ones, they pay four asses. The heirs of Firminus Secundus provide ninety-seven jugera of land and fifteen new ones, they pay three asses. Quinto Curtio Rufo, duovir and overseer, provides fifteen new jugera. The heirs of Firminus Secundus pay three asses.
EDCS-14000029.summary: This inscription records land distribution and payments made by the heirs of Firminus Secundus for the twenty-third cohort of volunteers of the colony of Vienna.

EDCS-14800020.text_edition: \n Titus Pompeius Titi libertus Palatina Phrixus Longus sevir Firmo Iulio et Copia Munatia Luguduni vivos sibi fecit Regini eius libertus ex sacroviri gente qui decessit Romae nnorum XVII\n
EDCS-14800020.translation: Titus Pompeius Phrixus Longus, freedman of Titus, of the Palatine tribe, a sevir, made this for himself while alive, with Reginus, his freedman, of the Sacroviri clan, who died in Rome at the age of seventeen.
EDCS-14800020.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Titus Pompeius Phrixus Longus, a freedman and sevir, by his freedman Reginus, who died young.

EDCS-15000126.text_edition: \n Teucer hic fuit ante diem IV Kalendas Apriles Cnaeo Domitio Caio Sossio consulibus\n
EDCS-15000126.translation: Teucer was here four days before the Kalends of April, in the consulship of Gnaeus Domitius and Gaius Sossius.
EDCS-15000126.summary: This is an epitaph or marker recording the presence of Teucer on a specific date in 49 BC.

EDCS-15700157.translation: Procellus Curmisso Albarino fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-15700157.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Procellus Curmisso Albarino fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-15800460.text_edition: \n Vivont sic dei Manes sacrum humane Titus Vettius Publi filius Papiria Loripes summae sic rudis Publius Vettius Titi filius Papiria Martialis duas sic Vettias sic Titi liberti Suavis et Utilis\n
EDCS-15800460.translation: The divine Manes (rest in peace). Titus Vettius, son of Publius, of the Papirian tribe, called Loripes (clubfoot), and his son Publius Vettius Martialis, son of Titus, of the Papirian tribe, and two slaves of Titus, freedmen, Suavis and Utilis (rest in peace).
EDCS-15800460.summary: This inscription is a funerary dedication for Titus Vettius, his son Publius Vettius Martialis, and two freedmen, Suavis and Utilis, likely related to a family tomb.

EDCS-15900138.text_edition: \n Viamae deae Craxo votum solvit libens merito voto excepto\n
EDCS-15900138.translation: To the goddess Viama, Crax willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow, with the exception of the vow.
EDCS-15900138.summary: This is a votive inscription by Crax to the goddess Viama, acknowledging the fulfillment of a vow.

EDCS-20500239.text_edition: \n Lucius Lucius C Lucius V Quintus \n
EDCS-20500239.translation: Lucius Lucius C Lucius V Quintus
EDCS-20500239.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely containing names, possibly a funerary or ownership marker.

EDCS-20500248.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Vetinia Nereis fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-20500252.translation: Vini Viventiaes, a pultarius (a type of vessel or possibly a person's name/nickname).
EDCS-20500252.summary: This inscription possibly records names or a designation related to a vessel, possibly of funerary context.

EDCS-20500256.text_edition: \n Cornelio Gabiniani filio Gabiniano Voltinia quaestori colonia Vienna flamini Iuventutis IIviro [iure dicundo ------\n
EDCS-20500256.translation: To Cornelius Gabinianus, son of Gabinianus, of the Voltinian tribe, quaestor, to Gabinianus, of the colony of Vienna, flamen of Youth, duovir [judiciary...].
EDCS-20500256.summary: This is a dedication to Cornelius Gabinianus, listing his various civic and religious offices in the colony of Vienna.

EDCS-20500266.text_edition: \n Marcus Iulius Magnus Marti ut voverat solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-20500266.translation: Marcus Iulius Magnus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Mars.
EDCS-20500266.summary: A private votive inscription from Marcus Iulius Magnus to Mars, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23900155.text_edition: \n Quintus Ateius Calventius Lucius Ateius Rufus Mercurio votum solverunt libentes merito quod vovit Calventius Capitonis filius\n
EDCS-23900155.translation: Quintus Ateius Calventius and Lucius Ateius Rufus willingly and deservedly fulfilled their vow to Mercury, which Calventius, son of Capito, had vowed.
EDCS-23900155.summary: A private votive inscription by Quintus Ateius Calventius and Lucius Ateius Rufus to Mercury, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-23900166.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Papiriae Luci filiae Tertull\n
EDCS-23900166.translation: To the Divine Shades of Papiria, daughter of Lucius, called Tertulla.
EDCS-23900166.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Papiria Tertulla, daughter of Lucius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23900170.text_edition: \n Mansuetae Mansueti filiae heredes faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-23900170.translation: Her heirs had this made for Mansueta, daughter of Mansuetus.
EDCS-23900170.summary: A funerary inscription commissioned by the heirs for Mansueta, daughter of Mansuetus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-23900171.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Macrini Tatia Macrina filio piissimo\n
EDCS-23900171.translation: To the Divine Shades of Macrina, to her most devoted son.
EDCS-23900171.summary: A funerary inscription dedicated to Macrina, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-24200263.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Viventia O filiae pientissimae vixit annos XVII mensis sic VI\n
EDCS-24200263.translation: To the Divine Shades of Viventia, daughter of O, most devoted, who lived 17 years and 6 months.
EDCS-24200263.summary: A funerary inscription for Viventia, daughter of O, detailing her age at death, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701422.summary: A name inscription, possibly funerary or indicating ownership, for Marcus Cornelius of Narbonne, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701426.summary: A fragmented inscription possibly related to a silversmith named Frugi, with unclear measurements or duration, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701432.text_edition: \n Licinius Elenus nius hic est sepultus\n
EDCS-28701432.summary: A simple funerary inscription stating that Licinius Elenus is buried here, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701443.text_edition: \n Publius Coius Publi Titi Gai! libertus Diocles pedes quoqueversus XV\n
EDCS-28701443.translation: Publius Coius Diocles, freedman of Publius Titius Gaius, [buried] fifteen feet in every direction.
EDCS-28701443.summary: A funerary inscription for Publius Coius Diocles, freedman of Publius Titius Gaius, specifying the dimensions of his burial plot, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701447.translation: Gaia Rufilla, freedwoman of Gaius, lives on!
EDCS-28701447.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Gaia Rufilla, a freedwoman, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701456.translation: Marcus Clodius Aestivo dedicated this to Tutela.
EDCS-28701456.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Tutela by Marcus Clodius Aestivo, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28701457.text_edition: \n Isidi Reginae Marcus Vipsanius Eucdemo votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-28701457.translation: Marcus Vipsanius Eucdemo willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Queen Isis.
EDCS-28701457.summary: This is a votive inscription where Marcus Vipsanius Eucdemo fulfilled a vow to the goddess Isis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-28800622.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmentary ownership mark or dedication, possibly related to Sextus Lepidus.

EDCS-28800623.summary: This inscription seems to be a fragmentary ownership mark or dedication, possibly related to Iulius Erotis.

EDCS-28800624.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary name, likely an ownership mark or part of a dedication, mentioning Marius, son of Sextus.

EDCS-28800625.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragmentary ownership mark or dedication, possibly related to Gaius Rutilius.

EDCS-28800630.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary name, likely an ownership mark or part of a dedication, belonging to Titus Afranius.

EDCS-28800634.translation: Of Quintus Cato, son of Quintus Cato Terentius.
EDCS-28800634.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary name, likely an ownership mark or part of a dedication, belonging to Quintus Cato, son of Quintus Cato Terentius.

EDCS-28800636.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary name, likely an ownership mark or part of a dedication, mentioning Maximi twice and an illegible portion.

EDCS-28800637.text_edition: \n Luci Caesi Severi procuratoris AAugustorum dominorum nostrorum probator Felix CCXCVIIII\n
EDCS-28800637.translation: Lucius Caelsius Severus, procurator of our August Emperors, Felix approved 299.
EDCS-28800637.summary: This inscription records the approval by Lucius Caelsius Severus, an imperial procurator, of a measurement or count of 299, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-28800643.translation: Cuintius, son of Lupiacus, crossed the Transalpine mountains.
EDCS-28800643.summary: This inscription, possibly a funerary or milestone marker, records the name Cuintius and refers to a crossing of the Transalpine mountains, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30101141.text_edition: \n Maxumus sic Adepta Nasso L Valerius Luci filius Pupinia pater Sec mater\n
EDCS-30101141.translation: Maxumus, son of Adeptus, and Nasso; Valerius, son of Lucius, father; Secundus, mother.
EDCS-30101141.summary: This inscription is a family record listing Maxumus, his parents Adeptus and Nasso, and Valerius (possibly a paternal figure) and his wife (Secundus, mother), likely from a Roman provincial context.

EDCS-30101144.summary: This inscription records the name Anecilius and his role as 'metretes', likely a local official or leader in a Roman provincial community.

EDCS-30101211.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Hermiae partiarii collegae posuerunt\n
EDCS-30101211.translation: His colleagues, the partiarii, set this up for the Divine Manes of Hermia.
EDCS-30101211.summary: This funerary inscription records that the colleagues of Hermia, who worked as 'partiarii' (likely estate managers or tax collectors), erected this monument to her memory.

EDCS-30101230.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius C Quinti filius I S S filius Q C R \n
EDCS-30101230.translation: To the Divine Manes, Lucius, son of Quintus. His son, Quintus, erected this.
EDCS-30101230.summary: This funerary inscription records the dedication to Lucius, son of Quintus, by his son, also named Quintus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30101235.text_edition: \n Magio Severino civi io Vergellense tribuno militum legionis II Italicae \n
EDCS-30101235.translation: To Magius Severinus, citizen of Vergi, tribune of soldiers of the II Italica legion.
EDCS-30101235.summary: This inscription honors Magius Severinus, a citizen of Vergi and a military tribune serving in the Legio II Italica, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30101243.text_edition: \n Cassius Theomnestus sibi et Cassio Aristoni vivus fecit hoc monumentum heredem non sequetur\n
EDCS-30101243.translation: Cassius Theomnestus made this monument for himself and for Cassius Aristo while alive; he will not be followed by an heir.
EDCS-30101243.summary: This funerary monument was erected by Cassius Theomnestus for himself and Cassius Aristo during their lifetimes, with the stipulation that no heir would inherit responsibility for it, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30101249.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Audentiae Alogius Mams filius Licin\n
EDCS-30101249.translation: To the Divine Manes, Audentia. Alogius, son of Mams, [erected this].
EDCS-30101249.summary: This funerary inscription is dedicated to Audentia and was erected by Alogius, son of Mams, likely from a Roman provincial context.

EDCS-30101250.text_edition: \n Manibus Saturnini et Amandae et Sabini\n
EDCS-30101250.translation: To the Manes of Saturninus and Amanda and Sabinus.
EDCS-30101250.summary: This funerary inscription is a dedication to the spirits of Saturninus, Amanda, and Sabinus, likely erected by a surviving family member or friend.

EDCS-30400514.translation: The work of Chresimus, freedman of Lucius Munatius Crescentis, from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-30400514.summary: This inscription records a doliare work (production of large jars) completed by Chresimus, likely a freedman, originating from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, dated by the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-30400517.translation: The work of Chresimus, freedman of Lucius Munatius Crescentis, from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-30400517.summary: This inscription records a doliare work (production of large jars) completed by Chresimus, likely a freedman, originating from the estates of Domitia Lucilla, dated by the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-30900105.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Lucconi Lucani Marcus Lucconius Paternus Marcus Lucconius Marcellus fratri Lucana M[--- filia ------\n
EDCS-30900105.translation: To the Divine Spirits. Marcus Lucconius Paternus and Marcus Lucconius Marcellus [erected this] for their brother, from Lucana [daughter...].
EDCS-30900105.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Spirits for Marcus Lucconius Paternus and Marcus Lucconius Marcellus, commemorating a deceased brother and mentioning a daughter named Lucana.

EDCS-31600218.translation: The doliare work of Quintus Oppius Verecundus, from the estates of Licinianis belonging to Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31600218.summary: This inscription records a doliare work (production of large jars) by Quintus Oppius Verecundus, originating from the Licinian estates of Domitia Lucilla, dated by the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31600219.translation: The doliare work of Anicetus, slave of Domitia Lucilla, from the Licinian estates, under the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.
EDCS-31600219.summary: This inscription records a doliare work (production of large jars) by Anicetus, a slave of Domitia Lucilla, originating from the Licinian estates, dated by the consulship of Paetinus and Apronianus.

EDCS-31600220.translation: The doliare work of Gnaeus Domitius Carpus, from the Licinian estates of Domitia Lucilla, under the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31600220.summary: This inscription records a doliare work (production of large jars) by Gnaeus Domitius Carpus, originating from the Licinian estates of Domitia Lucilla, dated by the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-31700117.translation: For Marcus Lurius Valentis, under the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.
EDCS-31700117.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or commission for Marcus Lurius Valentis, dated by the consulship of Apronianus and Paetinus.

EDCS-32800489.summary: This inscription is a simple naming and filiation, identifying Eumolpus as the son of Publius Calpurnius Atilianus.

EDCS-32805288.summary: This inscription is a simple naming of an individual, Munatius Treptus.

EDCS-32805289.summary: This inscription is a simple naming of an individual, Munatius Treptus.

EDCS-32900125.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sepulcrales
EDCS-32900125.text_edition: \n Lucio Terentio filio Pupinia Potito filio agenti annos XXVII decurioni ornamentario Claudia Luteva quaestori IIviro decurioni coloniae urbis Iuliae Baeterrensium Lucio Terentio Potito\n
EDCS-32900125.translation: To Lucius Terentius Potitus, son of Lucius Terentius, of the Pupinia tribe, who served as a decurion with ornamental rank, and Claudia Luteva, quaestor, twice duumvir, and decurion of the colony of the city of Julia Baeterrensis, [this was set up by] Lucius Terentius Potitus.
EDCS-32900125.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Lucius Terentius Potitus, likely set up by his father, who held several civic and military offices in Baeterrae.

EDCS-33400004.text_edition: \n Caio Oppio Voltinia Secundo militi cohortis III praetoriae centuria Luci Baebi Sabini militavit annos XVI optio annos III vixsit sic annos XL testamento fieri iussit\n
EDCS-33400004.translation: To Gaius Oppius Secundus, soldier of the 3rd Praetorian cohort, centuria of Lucius Baebius Sabinus. He served as a soldier for 16 years and as an optio for 3 years. He lived for 40 years. He ordered this to be made by his will.
EDCS-33400004.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Oppius Secundus, a soldier in the Praetorian Guard, who served for 19 years and died at the age of 40.

EDCS-33400010.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sammiae Placentinae collibertae et coniugi piissimae Sammius Primitivus vivus et sibi fecit et sub ascia dedicavit\n
EDCS-33400010.translation: To the Divine Shades. Sammia Placentina, freedwoman and most devoted wife. Sammius Primitivus, alive, made this for himself and dedicated it under the axe.
EDCS-33400010.summary: This is a funerary monument set up by Sammius Primitivus for his wife Sammia Placentina and himself, indicating it was consecrated and intended to be burned in a pyre.

EDCS-33500301.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cai Comini Cominiani Comini Connius et Connianus et Conniola filii patri pientissimo posuerunt et sub ascia dedicaverunt\n
EDCS-33500301.translation: To the Divine Shades. Cai Cominius Cominianus, his sons Cominius and Connianus and Conniola, to their most dutiful father, placed [this] and dedicated it under the axe.
EDCS-33500301.summary: A funerary dedication by three sons, Cominius, Connianus, and Conniola, to their very dutiful father, Cai Cominius Cominianus.

EDCS-33500339.text_edition: \n Publice ex decreto decurionum Marco Carisio Sexti filio Voltinia Alpino praefecto fabrum tribuno militum legionis XXI\n
